proposals.title: Assessing the Fidelity of Chromatin Architecture and Transcription Factor Dynamics in Digitonin-Permeabilized Live Cells
proposals.motivation: Digitonin permeabilization is a cornerstone technique for live-cell studies of nuclear processes, enabling controlled access to the nucleus for probes and enzymes. However, its impact on native chromatin organization, particularly at critical regulatory elements like CTCF-mediated boundaries and enhancer-promoter loops, remains uncharacterized. Given the pre-2026 understanding that such structures are essential for proper gene regulation and insulation, validating the technique's biological fidelity is crucial for interpreting a vast body of work in chromatin biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that digitonin permeabilization at concentrations standard for live-cell assays does not disrupt higher-order chromatin architecture or the dynamic binding of architectural proteins like CTCF and RNA polymerase II at promoters and boundaries.
proposals.method: We will use Drosophila S2 cells and a combination of CUT&RUN with antibodies against CTCF, histone modifications, and RNA Polymerase II to compare chromatin states before and after permeabilization. We will integrate this with live-cell imaging of endogenously tagged CTCF and Pol II using CRISPR/Cas9 to measure protein dynamics via FRAP. Specific computational analyses will include differential peak calling (MACS2) for CUT&RUN data and quantitative modeling of FRAP recovery curves to extract binding kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, systematic benchmark for the digitonin permeabilization method, defining its limits and reliability for chromatin studies. It will directly inform the interpretation of experiments on transcription, insulation, and enhancer function that rely on this technique, strengthening the foundation of the field.
proposals.novelty: While digitonin is widely assumed to be gentle, this study provides the first direct, multi-faceted test of its effects on native chromatin topology and the real-time dynamics of its key architectural components, bridging gaps between biochemical and live-cell observations.

proposals.title: Single-cell multimodal profiling of CTCF-mediated insulation and transcriptional escape during Drosophila spermatogenesis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including Henikoff's, shows that chromatin domain boundaries established by CTCF are crucial for gene regulation, yet how these boundaries are established and fail in a cell-type-specific manner, particularly in dynamic developmental contexts like spermatogenesis, remains unclear. Single-cell RNA-seq or ATAC-seq alone cannot directly link the binding of architectural proteins like CTCF to the transcriptional outcomes they regulate within the same cell.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cell-to-cell heterogeneity in CTCF occupancy, measured by single-cell CUT&Tag, will correlate directly with boundary strength and the probability of transcriptional escape of specific promoters in the developing Drosophila male germline.
proposals.method: We will perform combinatorial single-cell CUT&Tag for CTCF and RNA Polymerase II (Pol II) with single-nucleus RNA-seq (snRNA-seq) on Drosophila testes using a droplet-based platform (e.g., 10x Genomics Multiome ATAC + Gene Expression, adapted for CUT&Tag). Nuclei will be sequentially processed for CUT&Tag using protein A-Tn5 loaded with unique barcoded adapters for each target, followed by snRNA-seq library generation. Data integration will be performed using a custom analysis pipeline building on Seurat's Weighted Nearest Neighbor (WNN) method and chromVAR to link single-cell chromatin feature matrices with transcriptional profiles.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct, single-cell map linking architectural protein binding to transcriptional output in a developmental lineage, revealing how CTCF boundary establishment and failure are coordinated during cellular differentiation. It will establish a robust multimodal framework for integrating low-input epigenomic profiling with transcriptomics.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell multi-omics for ATAC+RNA exists, applying CUT&Tag for specific protein-DNA interactions in a true single-cell, multimodal format is novel. Combining this with Henikoff's focus on polymerase dynamics and chromatin maturation in spermatogenesis targets a unique biological question.

proposals.title: Nanobody-Targeted CUT&Tag for High-Resolution Mapping of Chromatin Boundary Complexes
proposals.motivation: Current methods for profiling protein-specific chromatin landscapes, like CUT&Tag which uses Protein A-Tn5 fusions, rely on large antibody conjugates that limit spatial resolution and accessibility to dense chromatin complexes. This is a critical limitation for studying the precise architecture of boundaries and insulator elements, where proteins like CTCF and RNA polymerase II are closely spaced and functionally interdependent. A smaller, more specific tethering system is needed to resolve these intricate interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing the Protein A moiety in Tn5 fusions with antigen-specific nanobodies will enable more efficient and higher-resolution mapping of chromatin-associated proteins, particularly at tightly packed regulatory elements like divergent promoters and CTCF-mediated boundaries.
proposals.method: We will engineer a fusion protein linking a hyperactive Tn5 transposase to a nanobody specific for the endogenous protein of interest (e.g., CTCF, RNA polymerase II). This nanobody-Tn5 construct will be used in a modified CUT&Tag protocol on Drosophila tissues and cell lines. We will compare the resulting chromatin maps to those generated by standard antibody-based CUT&Tag and ATAC-seq, assessing signal-to-noise, resolution at known boundaries, and the ability to detect co-occupancy at closely spaced divergent promoters identified in prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, broadly applicable tool for epigenomics that provides superior resolution for mapping protein-DNA interactions. It will yield new mechanistic insights into how CTCF boundary function is reinforced by local transcription and RNA, addressing long-standing questions in chromatin domain insulation. The method will be particularly valuable for studying compact genomic regions inaccessible to bulkier probes.
proposals.novelty: While nanobodies are established for imaging, their application to tether a DNA-modifying enzyme for targeted sequencing in chromatin represents a significant technical innovation. This approach moves beyond the one-size-fits-all Protein A system to a modular, protein-specific targeting strategy.

proposals.title: Systematic Profiling of CTCF-Depleted Chromatin Domains Reveals a Novel Class of Regulatory Boundaries in Drosophila Germ Cells
proposals.motivation: Chromatin profiling has focused on mapping the presence of marks, but the functional significance of their specific absence remains underexplored. In pre-2026 work, CTCF is known to be a key architectural protein and insulator, yet its genome-wide depletion patterns, particularly in specialized cell types like the germline, are not systematically defined. Understanding these 'negative spaces' could reveal novel principles of domain boundary formation and transcriptional regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genomic regions specifically and stably depleted of CTCF in the Drosophila male germline represent a functionally distinct class of chromatin boundaries, which facilitate promoter-enhancer interactions and transcriptional 'escape' in a manner complementary to CTCF-bound insulators.
proposals.method: We will adapt CUT&RUN, a method previously refined by the Henikoff lab, for negative selection by performing immunodepletion of CTCF-bound chromatin fragments prior to sequencing, thereby enriching for CTCF-depleted regions. We will integrate these maps with existing germline datasets on RNA Polymerase II dynamics, histone modifications, and chromosome conformation (Hi-C) from recent lab papers. A custom computational pipeline will identify significant depletion peaks, correlate them with transcriptional output from divergent promoters, and test for insulation score changes using established algorithms like HiCExplorer.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution map of a defined chromatin mark's absence, establishing a methodological framework for 'depletomics'. It will directly test the model from recent lab findings that active enhancers can strengthen insulation, by determining if CTCF-depleted boundaries represent sites of alternative, RNA-mediated insulation mechanisms. The results will redefine our understanding of chromatin domain architecture in gametogenesis.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard paradigm of chromatin immunoprecipitation by targeting what is not bound, a conceptually novel strategy. Applying this to CTCF in the germline context, where chromosome-specific epigenomic maturation occurs, investigates a non-obvious layer of regulation beyond canonical insulator maps.

proposals.title: Predicting functional CTCF-mediated boundaries by integrating sequence conservation with transcriptional co-regulation and protein interaction networks
proposals.motivation: CTCF binding sites are essential for chromatin domain insulation and gene regulation, but not all bound sites function as robust boundaries. Sequence conservation alone is an imperfect predictor of functional insulation. Pre-2026 work, including Henikoff's studies on boundary maturation and RNA-mediated CTCF binding, suggests that boundary function is influenced by local transcriptional context and co-factor interactions. A predictive framework that integrates these diverse data types is needed to distinguish constitutive from context-dependent boundaries.
proposals.hypothesis: Functional, evolutionarily conserved CTCF boundary elements are embedded within specific local transcriptional networks and are co-regulated with genes whose expression they insulate, a signature detectable by integrating genomic, transcriptomic, and interactome data.
proposals.method: We will develop a machine learning model (e.g., Random Forest or Gradient Boosting) trained on high-confidence functional boundaries from Drosophila and mammalian cell lines (defined by Hi-C, ChIP-seq, and STARR-seq). Features will include: 1) multi-species sequence conservation (PhyloP scores), 2) tissue-specific co-expression patterns of flanking genes (from RNA-seq), 3) promoter-proximal RNA polymerase II dynamics (PRO-seq), and 4) proximity in protein-protein interaction networks of CTCF co-factors (e.g., cohesin subunits). Model performance will be validated using CRISPR-mediated boundary deletion and subsequent assays for insulation failure and ectopic enhancer-promoter interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive algorithm for identifying functional insulation elements, moving beyond simple binding site annotation. It will reveal how transcriptional activity and protein networks contribute to boundary robustness, directly testing models of RNA-mediated CTCF binding. The results will offer a principled way to prioritize non-coding variants in boundary regions for functional studies in development and disease.
proposals.novelty: While sequence conservation and CTCF binding are commonly used, their integration with dynamic, cell-type-specific co-expression and physical interaction data to predict boundary function is a novel, systems-level approach. This directly builds on Henikoff's recent findings linking active transcription and RNA to boundary strength.

proposals.title: Testing the sufficiency of CTCF-mediated chromatin boundary re-engineering to substitute for oncogenic driver mutations in cellular transformation
proposals.motivation: Cancer progression involves both genetic driver mutations and widespread epigenetic alterations. A central, unresolved question is whether these epigenetic changes are merely permissive or can functionally substitute for key genetic lesions. This is critical for understanding tumor evolution and the potential of epigenetic therapies. Pre-2026 work, including Henikoff's on CTCF boundaries and enhancer insulation, provides tools to directly manipulate chromatin architecture, enabling a direct test of this principle.
proposals.hypothesis: Artificially re-engineering chromatin domain boundaries and promoter-enhancer interactions via targeted epigenetic editing is insufficient to replace the oncogenic function of specific driver mutations (e.g., in Ras or Myc) in cellular transformation assays.
proposals.method: We will use dCas9-based epigenetic editors (e.g., dCas9-p300, dCas9-DNMT3A) and programmable insulator disruptors (dCas9-CTCF fusions) in isogenic mammalian cell lines. We will first map existing chromatin domains and CTCF boundaries in cells with and without a defined driver mutation (e.g., KRAS G12D). Using this map, we will design targeted interventions to mimic the mutant's chromatin state in wild-type cells, specifically altering promoter-enhancer interactions at key oncogenes. We will employ CUT&RUN to verify epigenetic changes and RNA-seq to assess transcriptional outcomes. The functional sufficiency will be tested using established transformation assays (soft agar growth, xenograft models).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, causal test of the functional hierarchy between genetic and epigenetic changes in oncogenesis. It will clarify whether epigenetic therapies can potentially reverse a cancerous state or merely modulate it. The findings will advance our fundamental understanding of how chromatin architecture contributes to, but may not initiate, disease phenotypes.
proposals.novelty: While epigenetic alterations are known to co-occur with mutations, directly testing their sufficiency as replacements using precise, locus-specific chromatin engineering in an isogenic background is a novel and rigorous approach. It moves beyond correlation to causality in the genetic-epigenetic interplay of cancer.

proposals.title: Network fragility of chromatin domain boundaries underlies epigenetic dysregulation in tumor-progenitor cells
proposals.motivation: In cancer, epigenetic disruption of progenitor cell states is a key oncogenic driver, but the systemic principles governing this failure are poorly understood. Pre-2026 work, including from the Henikoff lab, establishes that CTCF-mediated boundaries and enhancer-promoter interactions are critical for proper gene regulation, and their perturbation is linked to disease. Modeling this system as a network can reveal why specific epigenetic lesions have catastrophic, tissue-specific consequences for cell identity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the epigenetic regulatory network in progenitor cells exhibits specific topological fragilities, where targeted disruption of highly connected boundary elements (e.g., CTCF/RNA-associated sites) leads to network failure, manifesting as the aberrant activation of tumor-progenitor gene programs.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution chromatin interaction maps (Hi-C), CTCF ChIP-seq, and RNA polymerase II occupancy data from normal and transformed progenitor cells to construct directed, weighted networks. Nodes will represent regulatory elements (promoters, enhancers, boundaries), with edges weighted by interaction frequency and functional data (e.g., polymerase run-on signals). We will apply graph theory metrics (betweenness centrality, community detection using the Louvain algorithm) to identify critical, fragile nodes. Perturbations (e.g., CRISPR-mediated CTCF deletion) will be performed to validate predictions of network collapse and gene misregulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systems-level framework for understanding epigenetic dysregulation in cancer, moving beyond cataloging individual mutations. It will identify a new class of 'network fragility' biomarkers—high-centrality boundary elements—whose disruption predisposes to oncogenic reprogramming. The project will also functionally integrate the roles of CTCF, RNA, and polymerase dynamics in maintaining network integrity.
proposals.novelty: While network approaches have been applied to gene regulation, this proposal uniquely focuses on the topological fragility of the *structural* epigenome (boundaries/insulators) in a disease context. It directly tests the hypothesis that boundary elements are not just passive barriers but are critical, load-bearing hubs whose failure drives transcriptional network collapse.

proposals.title: Engineering a CTCF-based Chromatin Boundary to Cap and Protect Linear DNA Vectors from Exonucleolytic Degradation
proposals.motivation: Linearized DNA vectors are essential for cloning and genome engineering but are vulnerable to exonucleolytic degradation, typically requiring specific 3' overhangs for protection. This constraint limits the choice of restriction enzymes and complicates cloning strategies. Drawing inspiration from cellular mechanisms that protect chromosome ends and define chromatin boundaries could enable a more versatile and robust vector system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a synthetic DNA element derived from a strong CTCF-binding site, when placed at the terminus of a linear vector, will recruit a protective protein complex that forms a stable cap, shielding the DNA end from exonucleases independently of terminal sequence overhangs.
proposals.method: We will engineer a linear vector by inserting a high-affinity, consensus CTCF-binding sequence at its termini. Using in vitro assays with purified exonuclease, we will test the stability of these capped vectors compared to uncapped blunt-end controls. We will employ chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP) with an anti-CTCF antibody to confirm protein recruitment and use DNase I footprinting to assess physical protection. Finally, we will test the functional utility of these capped vectors in standard ligation and cloning reactions in E. coli.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, biology-inspired principle for stabilizing linear DNA, reducing reliance on specific restriction enzymes. It will contribute to the field of synthetic biology by creating a new tool for DNA manipulation and provide fundamental insights into how protein-mediated capping can insulate DNA ends, analogous to chromatin boundary function.
proposals.novelty: While CTCF is known for its role in forming chromatin loop boundaries and insulating domains, its application as an engineered protective cap for synthetic DNA vectors is unexplored. This directly translates concepts of chromosomal insulation and telomere protection into a practical biotechnology tool.

proposals.title: In-situ targeted re-sequencing of chromatin domains using a hybrid single-molecule scaffold-and-enzyme platform
proposals.motivation: Current long-read sequencing technologies face a trade-off between read length and base-calling accuracy, particularly in repetitive regions and homopolymers that are common in eukaryotic genomes. This limitation impedes the precise mapping of chromatin domain boundaries, such as those defined by CTCF binding, and the analysis of complex regulatory regions like divergent promoters. A method that combines ultra-long scaffolding with targeted, high-accuracy verification on the same molecule is needed to resolve these structurally and functionally important genomic features.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a physically integrated, two-modality sequencing instrument can generate a complete, ultra-long scaffold of a single DNA molecule and then use that scaffold to direct localized enzymatic re-sequencing of specific regions, thereby producing a composite read of unprecedented length and accuracy suitable for analyzing chromatin architecture.
proposals.method: We will develop a nanofluidic device that first applies a deletion-based sequencing method (e.g., successive nick-labeling and imaging) to a stretched, immobilized DNA molecule to create a long-range physical map. This scaffold will then guide a co-localized, engineered helicase-polymerase complex to perform real-time, targeted sequencing-by-synthesis on the same molecule at pre-identified regions of low confidence or high biological interest, such as CTCF binding sites or promoter sequences. Custom alignment algorithms will dynamically integrate the two data streams to produce a final, corrected sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel sequencing paradigm that breaks the destructive vs. parallelized workflow conflict, enabling the accurate *de novo* assembly of complex genomic regions and haplotype-resolved chromatin domains. It will provide a powerful tool for directly correlating nucleotide sequence with epigenetic features like insulation and promoter activity on single molecules.
proposals.novelty: The physical integration of two orthogonal sequencing modalities on a single molecule for guided, in-situ re-sequencing is a non-obvious synthesis of existing concepts. This moves beyond software-based hybrid correction by performing the confirmation physically within the same instrument, preserving long-range molecular context.

proposals.title: A massively parallel CRISPR-Cas9 deletion strategy for high-throughput functional mapping of chromatin domain boundaries and enhancer-promoter interactions
proposals.motivation: Large-scale genomic sequencing projects, such as those mapping chromatin architecture via Hi-C, have identified thousands of candidate CTCF-boundary elements and enhancer-promoter pairs. However, functional validation of these elements remains a major bottleneck, as current methods for generating targeted deletion libraries are low-throughput and labor-intensive. A scalable, parallelized approach is urgently needed to systematically test the role of these non-coding elements in insulation, transcriptional regulation, and escape from silencing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pooled, multiplexed CRISPR-Cas9 deletion strategy, coupled with barcoded sequencing readouts, can be used to functionally screen hundreds of candidate boundary or enhancer elements in a single experiment, revealing quantitative effects on promoter activity and domain insulation.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale the Henikoff lab's expertise in targeted genomic perturbations and chromatin profiling. The method will utilize a pooled library of synthetic gRNA pairs designed to create precise deletions (200-2000 bp) at thousands of target loci, including CTCF sites and divergent promoter regions. This library will be delivered to Drosophila S2 cells or a model mammalian cell line via lentiviral transduction. Post-deletion, phenotypic effects will be assayed in parallel via RNA-seq (for transcriptional changes) and CUT&RUN for CTCF/Pol II (for chromatin changes), with each deletion event uniquely identified by molecular barcodes integrated during CRISPR repair.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, open-source pipeline for the high-throughput functional annotation of non-coding genomic elements. It will provide the first systematic, quantitative dataset on the contribution of individual CTCF sites to boundary strength and the rules governing enhancer-promoter specificity, directly informing models of 3D genome organization and gene regulation.
proposals.novelty: While pooled CRISPR screens exist for coding genes, applying this parallelized deletion logic specifically to non-coding regulatory elements at scale, and coupling it with chromatin-based readouts, is a novel integration of genomics and functional genomics tailored for the epigenetics field.

proposals.title: Causal role of RNA polymerase II pausing in establishing promoter-proximal histone modifications in Drosophila
proposals.motivation: A canonical model in epigenetics posits that histone modifications, such as H3K4me3 and H3K27ac, recruit regulatory complexes that influence RNA polymerase II (Pol II) pausing and release. However, the temporal order and causality remain ambiguous. Pre-2026 work, including Henikoff's studies on promoter-proximal chromatin and polymerase dynamics, suggests that transcription itself can shape the epigenetic landscape, challenging the direction of this relationship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the act of Pol II promoter-proximal pausing is a primary event that recruits histone-modifying enzymes, thereby establishing the local histone modification landscape, rather than modifications dictating pausing behavior.
proposals.method: We will use the Drosophila male germline system, leveraging its well-defined chromatin maturation timeline. We will acutely inhibit Pol II elongation with specific drugs like aclarubicin or triptolide in staged germ cells and track consequent changes in H3K4me3, H3K27ac, and H3K27me3 using CUT&RUN. In parallel, we will employ rapid degradation of the Integrator complex subunit to induce widespread Pol II pause release. High-resolution time-course analyses will determine if modification loss precedes or follows polymerase perturbation. Computational analysis will correlate pausing indices from PRO-seq data with modification dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the direction of causality between transcription and chromatin marks, a fundamental question in gene regulation. It will provide a mechanistic framework for how transcription shapes the epigenome during cellular differentiation, specifically in germline development.
proposals.novelty: While correlations between marks and pausing are well-established, this study uses acute, timed perturbations to invert the presumed causality, moving beyond observational studies to direct functional testing in a developing tissue context.

proposals.title: Nucleosome and DNA Methylation Landmarks Enforce Constitutive Exon Definition via RNA Polymerase II Elongation Control
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies show that chromatin features, like nucleosome positioning and histone modifications, can influence alternative splicing. However, the mechanisms ensuring constitutive exon inclusion—a fundamental requirement for gene fidelity—remain poorly understood. This gap is critical, as erroneous exon skipping is a major cause of genetic disease, and a chromatin-based 'splicing code' for constitutive exons has not been defined.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, conserved epigenetic features—namely positioned nucleosomes and DNA methylation peaks—act as insulatory boundaries that enforce constitutive exon inclusion by modulating RNA polymerase II elongation kinetics and preventing disruptive chromatin remodeling across the exon.
proposals.method: We will use Drosophila and mouse models, applying Henikoff's CUT&RUN and enzymatic methyl-seq to profile nucleosome occupancy and DNA methylation at constitutive exons identified from existing transcriptomes. We will then perform targeted epigenetic editing using dCas9 fusions to disrupt these features at model loci and measure effects on RNA splicing via long-read sequencing. Crucially, we will employ PRO-seq to assess changes in Pol II elongation dynamics directly, and test for CTCF/cohesin involvement using auxin-inducible degrons, given its known role in boundary function and potential RNA-mediated recruitment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal link between specific epigenetic landmarks and the faithful inclusion of constitutive exons, moving beyond correlation. It will define a novel, conserved mechanism of splicing fidelity that integrates chromatin architecture with transcription elongation. The findings could reveal new pathogenic mechanisms for splicing mutations that occur outside canonical splice sites.
proposals.novelty: While chromatin's role in alternative splicing is recognized, its function in enforcing constitutive splicing is unexplored. The proposal uniquely tests epigenetic features as active insulatory boundaries for splicing, directly connecting concepts of chromatin domain insulation (CTCF) to the fundamental problem of exon definition.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Roles of CpG Density and DNA Bendability in Nucleosome Exclusion and DNA Methylation at Chromatin Domain Boundaries
proposals.motivation: CTCF-bound chromatin domain boundaries are critical for genome organization and gene regulation, yet the relative contributions of sequence features that establish these boundaries remain unclear. Pre-2026 work shows that CpG islands (CGIs) are often nucleosome-depleted and hypomethylated, but it is unresolved whether this is primarily due to high CpG density, which can inhibit DNA methylation, or to intrinsic DNA bendability, which influences nucleosome positioning. Understanding this distinction is key to deciphering the establishment of epigenetic states at regulatory elements like promoters and insulators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that at CTCF-bound boundaries, high CpG density is the primary driver of DNA hypomethylation, while local DNA bendability, as predicted by sequence-based models, is the dominant factor determining nucleosome exclusion.
proposals.method: We will use Drosophila and mouse embryonic stem cell models to perform high-resolution MNase-seq and bisulfite-seq on wild-type and CTCF-depleted cells, focusing on boundary regions. We will computationally partition boundaries based on CpG density and predicted DNA bendability scores (e.g., using the bend.it server or trinucleotide models). To test causality, we will use CRISPR/Cas9 to swap high- and low-bendability DNA sequences within synthetic reporter constructs integrated at defined genomic loci and assess nucleosome occupancy and methylation changes via CUT&RUN and targeted bisulfite sequencing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, sequence-level understanding of how two fundamental DNA properties independently shape the chromatin landscape at boundaries. It will clarify the causal hierarchy of sequence features in establishing the 'pre-pattern' for CTCF binding and epigenetic marking, directly informing models of insulator function and genome evolution.
proposals.novelty: While CpG density and DNA mechanics are both known to influence chromatin, their differential and potentially independent contributions at defined regulatory elements like CTCF boundaries have not been systematically dissected using integrated computational and direct genetic perturbation approaches.

proposals.title: Polymer Physics Modeling of RNA Polymerase II Elongation Dynamics Across CTCF-Mediated Chromatin Boundaries
proposals.motivation: CTCF-mediated boundaries are crucial for genome organization and gene regulation, but the physical mechanisms by which elongating RNA Polymerase II (Pol II) navigates these structures remain poorly understood. Recent work, including from our lab, shows that active enhancers can strengthen boundary insulation via RNA-mediated CTCF binding, and that drugs like aclarubicin can alter Pol II elongation at promoters, highlighting a dynamic interplay between transcription and chromatin architecture that demands a physical model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the success of Pol II elongation through a boundary element is governed by the dynamic, force-dependent competition between the polymerase's processive motion and the cohesive, loop-extruding activity of CTCF-cohesin complexes, which can be captured by a non-equilibrium polymer physics model.
proposals.method: We will develop a coarse-grained polymer model of chromatin, treating the fiber as a self-avoiding chain with bound CTCF molecules that mediate transient loops via slip-links. Pol II will be modeled as a molecular motor that exerts force and locally unwinds the fiber. Using Brownian dynamics simulations (e.g., with the OpenMM or LAMMPS frameworks), we will simulate elongation through boundary regions parameterized with genomic data from Drosophila and mammalian cells, specifically incorporating epigenetic peak data (e.g., CUT&RUN for CTCF, Pol II, histones) to define boundary strengths and polymerase densities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic framework to predict transcriptional outcomes at domain boundaries, linking epigenetic marks and protein binding to physical forces. It will directly test how perturbations like enhancer activity or small molecules that alter Pol II kinetics (e.g., aclarubicin) modulate boundary bypass, offering testable predictions for genome engineering and disease contexts of boundary dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While polymer models have described static chromatin folding, applying them to explicitly simulate the non-equilibrium process of Pol II elongation through characterized boundary elements, integrating real epigenetic and transcription factor binding data, is a novel synthesis of biophysics and functional genomics.

proposals.title: Predictive modeling of chromatin barrier function: integrating sequence, CTCF binding, and transcription to forecast nucleosome and DNA methylation landscapes
proposals.motivation: The precise positioning of nucleosomes and DNA methylation is crucial for gene regulation, chromatin domain insulation, and promoter function, yet predictive models remain incomplete. Pre-2026 work, including Henikoff's studies on CTCF boundaries and promoter-proximal polymerase dynamics, highlights that these features are interdependent, governed by sequence, transcription, and protein binding. A unified predictive framework is needed to understand how these factors collectively establish chromatin architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the genomic locations of nucleosome and DNA methylation peaks can be accurately predicted by a model integrating DNA sequence motifs, CTCF/cohesin binding dynamics, and real-time RNA polymerase II elongation states, with predictive power greatest at chromatin domain boundaries and active promoters.
proposals.method: We will train a hybrid convolutional and recurrent neural network (CNN-RNN) on high-resolution Drosophila and mouse datasets, including CUT&RUN for histone modifications, bisulfite sequencing for methylation, and PRO-seq for polymerase dynamics. Sequence features (k-mers, motif scores), chromatin features (CTCF, H3K4me3, H3K36me3), and transcriptional features (divergent promoter spacing, elongation rates) will be input. The model will be validated by comparing predictions to held-out genomic regions and tested via genetic perturbation of CTCF or polymerase function.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a generalizable model for predicting chromatin state formation, directly testing the relative contributions of sequence, insulation, and transcription. It will provide new mechanistic insights into how boundary elements like CTCF coordinate local nucleosome positioning and methylation, bridging the gap between epigenetic mapping and functional prediction.
proposals.novelty: While previous models often treat sequence or single epigenetic marks in isolation, our approach uniquely integrates real-time transcriptional dynamics and insulator biology—key themes from Henikoff's recent work—as direct predictive features for nucleosome and methylation landscapes.

proposals.title: Nucleotide-resolution mapping of RNA polymerase II pausing dynamics at chromatin domain boundaries and divergent promoters
proposals.motivation: Precise RNA polymerase II (Pol II) pausing is a critical, rate-limiting step in transcription regulation, influencing gene expression, enhancer-promoter communication, and co-transcriptional processes. However, existing methods like PRO-seq and NET-seq, while powerful, have limitations in resolution, sensitivity, or scalability for mapping pausing genome-wide at single-nucleotide precision, particularly in complex genomic contexts like CTCF boundaries and closely spaced divergent promoters. A method with the simplicity and resolution of ribosome profiling for Pol II would transform our understanding of transcriptional control.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the core principles of ribosome profiling—nuclease protection of engaged complexes followed by deep sequencing of protected fragments—to engaged RNA polymerase II will enable robust, nucleotide-resolution mapping of Pol II pausing. We hypothesize that this will reveal novel, sequence- and chromatin-dependent pausing signatures at architectural elements like CTCF boundaries and divergent promoters.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CUT&RUN platform, recently used by Henikoff's lab for chromatin profiling, to isolate engaged Pol II complexes. Specifically, we will use a specific antibody against the initiating form of Pol II (phospho-Ser5) in permeabilized nuclei, followed by cleavage with micrococcal nuclease (MNase) to release short, protected DNA fragments bound by the polymerase. These fragments, representing the polymerase's footprint, will be subjected to high-throughput sequencing. We will develop a dedicated computational pipeline, adapting algorithms from ribosome profiling (like periodicity filters) and CUT&RUN analysis, to map the 3' ends of protected fragments to the genome at single-nucleotide resolution, inferring Pol II occupancy and pausing sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, high-resolution method (Pol II Profiling) for mapping transcriptionally engaged polymerases, providing a new tool for the field. It will yield foundational insights into how Pol II pausing is regulated at chromatin domain boundaries and promoters, directly testing models from recent work on CTCF/RNA-mediated insulation and drug-stimulated elongation at divergent promoters.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and technical translation of the ribosome profiling framework to RNA polymerase II, leveraging the CUT&RUN platform for targeted, high-efficiency isolation of protein-nucleosome complexes, which is distinct from existing Pol II sequencing methods that rely on run-on or immunoprecipitation of large complexes.

proposals.title: Integrated analysis of CTCF-mediated insulation, RNA polymerase II dynamics, and alternative splicing in human chromatin domains
proposals.motivation: In Drosophila, the Henikoff lab has shown that RNA-mediated CTCF binding at boundaries is crucial for enhancer-blocking insulation and is influenced by active transcription. Mammalian genomes, however, are vastly more complex, featuring larger introns, pervasive alternative splicing, and intricate chromatin folding. It is unknown how these features, especially the coupling of transcription elongation and splicing, intersect with CTCF function to shape functional chromatin architecture in human cells.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in mammalian genomes, CTCF-mediated boundary strength and chromatin domain integrity are co-regulated by RNA polymerase II elongation dynamics and the recruitment of splicing factors, with alternative splicing events serving as modulators of local chromatin insulation.
proposals.method: We will perform an integrated analysis in human cell lines (e.g., K562, H1-ESC) using: 1) CUT&RUN for high-resolution mapping of CTCF, cohesin, and histone modifications; 2) Precision Run-On sequencing (PRO-seq) to map engaged RNA polymerase II and quantify elongation dynamics; and 3) long-read Iso-Seq (PacBio) coupled with standard RNA-seq to comprehensively define full-length transcript isoforms and splicing events. We will develop a computational pipeline to correlate boundary CTCF occupancy and insulation scores (from Hi-C data) with polymerase pausing indices, intron length, and the density of alternative splicing events across domain boundaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, mechanistic link between transcriptional elongation, co-transcriptional splicing, and the maintenance of chromatin domain boundaries in humans. It will reveal whether specific splicing factors or nascent RNA structures contribute to CTCF's insulator function, providing a unified model of gene regulation that integrates nuclear architecture with mRNA processing.
proposals.novelty: While CTCF's role as an insulator is known, its functional interplay with the kinetics of RNA polymerase II elongation and the splicing machinery within complex human introns at domain boundaries remains unexplored. This moves beyond correlative studies in simpler genomes to test mechanistic coupling in a more relevant genomic context.

proposals.title: CTCF and RNA polymerase II occupancy define functional boundaries at long non-coding RNA loci
proposals.motivation: CTCF binding and RNA polymerase II (Pol II) elongation dynamics are established as critical for forming chromatin boundaries and regulating promoter-proximal pausing at protein-coding genes. However, it is unknown whether long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) genes, which are abundant and often tissue-specific, utilize similar epigenetic and transcriptional architectures to establish functional domains and regulate their expression. Determining if lncRNA loci harbor boundary-associated marks would reveal fundamental principles of genome organization beyond coding regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of lncRNA genes, particularly those with divergent transcription or enhancer-like activity, will exhibit CTCF binding and specific Pol II elongation patterns at their boundaries, functionally insulating their transcriptional units from neighboring regulatory elements.
proposals.method: We will perform CUT&RUN in Drosophila S2 cells and larval tissues to map CTCF, cohesin (Rad21), and histone modifications (H3K4me3, H3K27ac) at high resolution across annotated lncRNA loci. This will be integrated with precision nuclear run-on sequencing (PRO-seq) data to profile Pol II occupancy and elongation dynamics. We will develop a computational pipeline to identify boundary-associated peaks at lncRNA TSSs and termini, using established algorithms for peak calling (MACS2) and domain analysis (HiCExplorer) on available Hi-C data. Candidate boundaries will be functionally validated using CRISPR/Cas9-mediated deletion followed by PRO-seq and RNA-seq.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish whether lncRNA genes are organized by canonical chromatin boundary mechanisms, potentially redefining them as integral architectural features of the genome. It will provide a high-resolution map of lncRNA-associated insulator elements and link specific Pol II states to boundary function at non-coding loci.
proposals.novelty: While CTCF boundaries at protein-coding genes are well-studied, a systematic investigation focusing on lncRNA loci as potential boundary elements is lacking. The proposal uniquely integrates Henikoff's expertise in Pol II dynamics and chromatin profiling to test if transcriptional mechanisms of insulation are conserved in non-coding genomic regions.

proposals.title: Whole-proteome chromatin factor binding site prediction using evolutionary constraint matrices derived from protein domain alignments
proposals.motivation: Current methods for predicting transcription factor and chromatin protein binding sites, like CTCF, often rely on position weight matrices (PWMs) derived from limited sequence alignments, which can miss deep evolutionary constraints. Pre-2026, large-scale protein domain databases (e.g., Pfam, InterPro) and structural fold classifications provide a rich, untapped source of evolutionary information that could reveal nucleotide-level constraints at binding sites, as protein-DNA interactions impose specific selective pressures on the DNA sequence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that creating whole-proteome alignment matrices (WPAMs) from blocks of conserved protein domains and folds will reveal nucleotide-level evolutionary signatures that significantly improve the prediction and functional annotation of chromatin factor binding sites, particularly for architectural proteins like CTCF.
proposals.method: We will extract all conserved blocks from Pfam and CATH databases to create multi-species protein sequence alignments. Using the codeml program from the PAML suite, we will infer site-specific evolutionary rates (dN/dS) for each alignment column. These rates will be mapped back to the corresponding genomic DNA sequences via the protein-coding exons, generating nucleotide-level evolutionary constraint profiles. These WPAMs will be integrated with existing chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP-seq) and promoter-proximal polymerase data using a machine learning framework (e.g., a random forest or gradient boosting model) to predict binding sites and classify functional boundaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, evolutionarily grounded resource (WPAMs) for the chromatin field, moving beyond simple sequence motifs. It will deliver a predictive model with higher accuracy for identifying functional CTCF boundaries and promoter elements, directly testing the relationship between deep protein evolutionary constraint and cis-regulatory function.
proposals.novelty: While protein evolution is well-studied, its systematic translation into nucleotide-level constraint matrices for genome-wide chromatin analysis is novel. This approach uniquely leverages the scale of whole-proteome domain alignments, rather than gene-centric alignments, to inform regulatory genomics.

proposals.title: Mapping Nuclease-Specific Genomic Rearrangements at Chromatin Boundaries Using Endpoint Capture and High-Throughput Sequencing
proposals.motivation: Understanding how nucleases, including non-specific nucleases like DNase I and sequence-specific engineered nucleases, reshape the genome is crucial for interpreting chromatin accessibility assays and the outcomes of genome editing. Current methods, such as ATAC-seq or analysis of CRISPR-Cas9 off-targets, often infer cleavage sites indirectly or require complex enrichment strategies, lacking a direct, high-resolution method to sequence the unique deletion endpoints generated by a given nuclease's activity across the genome.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ligating a common adapter sequence directly to nuclease-cleaved genomic ends, followed by endpoint-specific PCR amplification and high-throughput sequencing, will enable the direct, genome-wide identification and quantification of nuclease-specific deletion spectra, revealing cleavage preferences and rearrangement patterns with single-base resolution.
proposals.method: We will develop Nuclease-Specific Endpoint Amplification (NSEA) by treating isolated Drosophila or mammalian nuclei with a target nuclease (e.g., DNase I, Benzonase, or an engineered nuclease). Cleaved ends will be repaired and ligated to a biotinylated, double-stranded adapter with a known sequence. Following fragmentation and size selection, fragments containing the adapter will be captured using streptavidin beads. A two-step PCR will then be performed: the first using an adapter-specific primer and a primer targeting a region of interest (e.g., near a CTCF boundary or active promoter), and the second adding full Illumina sequencing adapters and sample indexes for multiplexed, high-throughput sequencing. Bioinformatics pipelines will map reads to identify precise deletion endpoints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable tool for directly profiling the genomic rearrangement landscape induced by any nuclease. It will yield new insights into how nuclease accessibility correlates with chromatin domain boundaries and transcriptional states, and establish a functional assay for characterizing the specificity and off-target effects of engineered nucleases.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing indirect assays for nuclease sensitivity or off-target prediction, NSEA directly sequences the deletion endpoints created by nuclease cleavage, bypassing cloning and enabling multiplexed, quantitative analysis of rearrangement spectra specific to a given enzyme's biochemical activity in its native chromatin context.

proposals.title: Mapping chromatin domain boundaries and promoter-proximal architecture using DNase I and MNase-seq in parallel
proposals.motivation: Micrococcal nuclease (MNase) has a strong sequence and chromatin-state bias, preferentially cleaving linker DNA and under-representing nucleosome-depleted regions like active promoters and boundaries. This limits the resolution and accuracy of chromatin mapping studies, particularly for understanding how CTCF boundaries and divergent promoters, as studied in Henikoff's recent work, are established and function. A comparative nuclease approach is needed to obtain a more complete architectural picture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing DNase I, which cleaves accessible DNA with less sequence bias, in parallel with MNase-seq will reveal novel, nuclease-sensitive features at chromatin domain boundaries and divergent promoters that are critical for their insulation and transcriptional regulatory functions.
proposals.method: We will perform parallel chromatin profiling in Drosophila S2 cells using MNase-seq and DNase I-seq with high-resolution sequencing. Data will be analyzed using the Henikoff lab's established pipelines for mapping nucleosome positions and accessible regions. We will specifically apply algorithms like DANPOS for dynamic nucleosome analysis and FIMO for motif discovery to compare boundary-associated footprints. Focused analyses will target CTCF sites and the closely spaced divergent promoters studied in the lab's aclarubicin work to identify nuclease-specific cleavage signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a more comprehensive and unbiased map of chromatin architecture at regulatory elements. It will directly test whether boundary insulation strength, potentially mediated by RNA-bound CTCF, correlates with specific nuclease accessibility patterns. The study will establish a dual-nuclease framework for future investigations into chromatin maturation and polymerase dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While MNase-seq is standard, systematic comparison with DNase I to dissect boundary and promoter mechanisms in a well-defined model system is not routine. Applying this direct comparative approach specifically to questions of CTCF-mediated insulation and divergent promoter topology, central to the lab's themes, represents a novel methodological and biological synthesis.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Control of Nuclear Access Enables In-Cell CUT&RUN for Mapping Chromatin Boundaries in Intact Cells
proposals.motivation: Current CUT&RUN and CUT&Tag protocols require cell permeabilization or nuclear isolation, disrupting native cellular context and potentially altering chromatin states. This is a critical limitation for studying dynamic processes like insulator function and enhancer-promoter communication, where cytoplasmic signaling and nuclear envelope integrity may influence chromatin architecture. A method to perform targeted chromatin profiling in truly intact cells would preserve these physiological conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: That engineering a photoactivatable pA-MNase fusion protein, sequestered in the cytoplasm via a caged nuclear localization signal (NLS) and a constitutive nuclear export signal (NES), will enable precise, UV-triggered nuclear import to perform efficient chromatin cleavage following antibody binding in live cells, thereby enabling high-resolution mapping of chromatin features like CTCF boundaries without prior nuclear isolation.
proposals.method: We will engineer a recombinant pA-MNase fusion protein with a caged NLS (using the LOV2 domain or a photocleavable masking peptide) and a constitutive SV40 NES. This construct will be expressed in or loaded into cells via streptolysin O (SLO) pores. After binding of primary antibodies to target antigens (e.g., CTCF, RNA Pol II), a brief, localized UV pulse will uncage the NLS, triggering rapid nuclear import via the importin pathway. Nuclear MNase activity will then be activated by Ca2+ addition to cleave antibody-bound chromatin, followed by standard extraction and sequencing. We will optimize UV dosage and timing using live-cell imaging of a GFP-tagged construct.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust 'In-Cell CUT&RUN' protocol that maintains full cellular integrity during chromatin profiling. It will allow us to test, for the first time in unperturbed cells, how cytoplasmic-nuclear compartmentalization influences insulator boundary strength and the establishment of chromatin domains, directly addressing themes from our recent work on RNA-mediated CTCF binding and chromosome-specific epigenome maturation.
proposals.novelty: While chemically inducible dimerization systems exist, the specific application of a photo-caged NLS/NES shuttle to spatially and temporally control the nuclear access of a chromatin-cleaving enzyme for epigenomic profiling in live cells is novel. This approach directly solves the problem of cytoplasmic entrapment and degradation that plagues attempts to use pA-MNase in intact cells.

proposals.title: Selective Chromatin Retention via dCas9-Directed Degradation for High-Resolution Mapping of Insulator Function
proposals.motivation: Current methods for mapping chromatin domain boundaries and insulator elements, such as CTCF sites, often rely on chromatin release or solubilization strategies (e.g., ChIP, Hi-C). These approaches can be inefficient for capturing transient or low-occupancy interactions and may dilute critical regulatory contexts. A method to degrade non-target chromatin in situ would instead enrich for specific genomic loci and their associated architectural proteins within their native nuclear environment, providing a more sensitive and structurally informative readout.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted degradation of non-specific chromatin via a nuclease-dead Cas9 (dCas9) fused to a chromatin-disrupting enzyme will selectively retain and enrich CTCF-bound insulator complexes, enabling superior mapping of boundary elements and their associated RNA and protein factors.
proposals.method: We will fuse dCas9 to the chromatin-remodeling ATPase domain of ISWI or to a potent histone degron (e.g., H2B-targeting mini-AID), guided by sgRNAs tiling regions flanking known and putative CTCF sites in Drosophila S2 cells. Following targeted degradation and wash-away of non-retained chromatin, we will perform ultra-low-input CUT&RUN (using Henikoff lab expertise) on the retained nuclear material to map CTCF, cohesin, and histone modifications. Parallel RNA-seq will assess transcriptional 'escape' at boundaries. Validation will involve comparison to standard ChIP-seq and Hi-C data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel 'Retention-ChIP' methodology that provides higher-resolution maps of insulator complex composition and function. It will directly test the model that RNA-mediated CTCF binding strengthens insulation at active enhancers. The technique will be a generalizable tool for studying any chromatin-associated factor in its structural context.
proposals.novelty: The strategy inverts the standard paradigm of target release by actively removing background chromatin, a concept not applied to insulator mapping. It uniquely combines dCas9-targeted degradation with ultra-sensitive epigenomic profiling from retained nuclear structures.

proposals.title: A Programmable dCas9 Scaffold for Simultaneous Recruitment of Multiple Effectors to Dissect Chromatin Domain Boundaries
proposals.motivation: Understanding how CTCF boundaries insulate chromatin domains and regulate enhancer-promoter communication is a central problem in epigenetics. Current CRISPR-based tools, like dCas9 fusions, are limited to recruiting a single effector per locus, making it difficult to probe the simultaneous and cooperative actions of multiple chromatin regulators at boundaries. A method to co-recruit distinct enzymatic activities to a single genomic site would enable more sophisticated functional dissection of these complex regulatory elements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering a dCas9 protein as a central scaffold with orthogonal peptide interaction domains will enable the efficient, simultaneous recruitment of multiple chromatin effectors to a defined genomic locus, allowing for the combinatorial manipulation and analysis of boundary function.
proposals.method: We will engineer a dCas9 scaffold fused to a set of orthogonal peptide interaction domains (e.g., SH3, PDZ, GBD). Effector proteins (e.g., a fluorescent histone for live imaging, a histone acetyltransferase like p300, and a nuclease for cleavage assays) will be tagged with the cognate binding peptides. Using a single, target-specific guide RNA at a CTCF-bound boundary, we will test the scaffold's ability to co-recruit all three effectors. Recruitment efficiency and functional outcomes (acetylation, transcription changes, boundary disruption) will be measured using CUT&RUN, RNA-seq, and live-cell imaging in Drosophila S2 cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a versatile platform for multi-parameter chromatin profiling and manipulation from a single targeting event. It will provide direct functional insights into how concurrent enzymatic activities at boundaries, such as histone modification and physical cleavage, cooperate to regulate insulation and transcription, directly testing models from recent studies on RNA-mediated CTCF binding.
proposals.novelty: While protein scaffolds are used in metabolic engineering, their application to create a multi-effector dCas9 platform for chromatin biology is novel. This moves beyond existing one-enzyme-per-dCas9 systems to enable combinatorial perturbation that mirrors the natural complexity of regulatory loci.

proposals.title: Chemically Inducible Sequential Chromatin Digestion and Tagmentation for High-Resolution Mapping of Insulator and Escapee Promoter Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Current methods for mapping chromatin accessibility and nucleosome positioning, such as ATAC-seq and MNase-seq, are limited by their single-step nature, which can obscure the sequential order of events at regulatory elements. This is particularly problematic for studying dynamic boundaries and promoters, such as CTCF sites and transcriptionally 'escapee' promoters in germline or developmental contexts, where precise nucleosome organization relative to DNA accessibility is critical for function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a chemically inducible dimerization (CID) system to temporally separate MNase digestion from Tn5-mediated tagmentation will yield superior resolution of chromatin architecture, enabling the unambiguous mapping of nucleosome-depleted regions and flanking nucleosome positions at regulatory boundaries and promoters in a single assay.
proposals.method: We will engineer separate constructs expressing MNase fused to FKBP12 and Tn5 transposase fused to FRB. In vitro, chromatin will first be digested with the MNase-FKBP12 fusion under optimized conditions. The addition of rapamycin will then induce rapid dimerization with Tn5-FRB, recruiting it specifically to the MNase-generated ends for synchronized tagmentation and library preparation. We will validate the system using Drosophila S2 cells and male germline nuclei, focusing on known CTCF boundaries and promoters exhibiting transcriptional escape.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified biochemical tool for high-resolution, sequential chromatin profiling. It will directly contribute to understanding how insulator boundaries are enforced and how promoter architecture facilitates transcriptional escape, bridging themes from Henikoff's recent work on chromosome-specific epigenome maturation and RNA-mediated CTCF binding.
proposals.novelty: While CID systems are established in chemical biology, their application to decouple and control the sequential enzymatic steps of a core chromatin biochemistry assay is novel. This moves beyond simple protein fusions to offer precise temporal control unavailable in existing commercial or published Tn5-MNase hybrid protocols.

proposals.title: Mapping the Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Nuclease-Induced DNA Breaks at CTCF Boundaries Using Light-Activated Super-Resolution Trapping
proposals.motivation: CTCF boundaries are critical for chromatin domain insulation and gene regulation, but how they resist or influence DNA damage and repair is poorly understood. Pre-2026 methods like ChIP-seq and CUT&RUN provide static maps, failing to capture the rapid, transient dynamics of nuclease activity at these sites in their native nuclear context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CTCF boundaries act as kinetic barriers to nuclease progression, and that rapid, sequential trapping of cleavage events will reveal distinct spatiotemporal patterns of DNA break formation and resolution at these sites compared to boundary-flanking regions.
proposals.method: We will adapt single-molecule localization microscopy (SMLM) for kinetic trapping. A light-activated, caged nuclease (e.g., Cas9) will be introduced into Drosophila cells with endogenously tagged CTCF. A brief UV pulse will synchronously activate cleavage, followed by rapid, millisecond-scale fixation via a co-activated crosslinker (e.g., a photoswitchable formaldehyde analog). Sequential snapshots of DNA breaks, labeled via γH2AX or direct oligonucleotide probes, will be captured using SMLM. Computational alignment of thousands of single-molecule break events across multiple nuclei and time points will generate high-resolution spatiotemporal probability maps of cleavage relative to CTCF loci.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct visualization of how architectural proteins like CTCF influence DNA damage kinetics in live cells. It will establish a novel methodology for correlating nanoscale chromatin structure with dynamic enzymatic activity, moving beyond static sequencing-based assays.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying the 'time-resolved trapping' concept from structural biology to live-cell super-resolution imaging of nuclease dynamics, specifically targeting the functional interplay between chromatin boundaries and DNA break mechanics.

proposals.title: Mapping Condition-Specific Eviction of CTCF and RNA Polymerase II Cohesin Complexes at Chromatin Boundaries
proposals.motivation: Chromatin boundaries, enforced by CTCF and cohesin, are dynamic structures critical for transcription and insulation. Pre-2026 work, including Henikoff's studies on CTCF-RNA interactions and promoter-proximal polymerase dynamics, shows these complexes are regulated, but the specific protein assemblies evicted upon boundary disruption remain unknown. A method to directly identify these evicted complexes would reveal the proteomic basis of chromatin domain stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific pharmacological or transcriptional perturbations, such as aclarubicin-induced polymerase elongation or loss of enhancer RNA, will trigger the eviction of distinct, multi-protein complexes from CTCF/cohesin-bound boundary elements, and that these evictees can be distinguished from non-specific background by differential proteomics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CUT&RUN technique for differential eviction proteomics. In Drosophila or mammalian cells, we will perform paired purifications: one under native conditions (DNA-bound) and one after inducing eviction (e.g., with aclarubicin or transcriptional inhibition). Using biotinylated dCas9 or an antibody (e.g., anti-CTCF) to tether complexes to beads, we will elute and compare evicted proteins via quantitative mass spectrometry (SILAC or TMT). The differential filter will subtract proteins common to both conditions, isolating high-confidence evictees. Validation will involve ChIP-seq for occupancy changes and functional assays of boundary integrity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-confidence map of protein complexes whose chromatin association is directly regulated at boundaries. It will identify novel cofactors of CTCF/cohesin that stabilize or dismantle insulation in response to transcription. The methodology will establish a generalizable framework for studying condition-specific protein eviction from any genomic locus.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods like BioID or ChIP-MS map stable associations, this approach uniquely focuses on the dynamic *loss* of complexes, using a paired, differential design to eliminate background. It directly tests the model from Henikoff's recent work that active enhancers and polymerase dynamics regulate boundary protein interactions.

proposals.title: Mapping Condition-Specific Eviction of Chromatin Insulators via Comparative Proximity Labeling at CTCF Boundaries
proposals.motivation: Chromatin domain boundaries, often defined by CTCF binding, are crucial for gene regulation, but how their associated protein complexes dynamically disassemble in response to stimuli remains poorly understood. Standard chromatin capture methods identify stable interactions but are poorly suited to detect the loss of factors, a critical regulatory event. This gap limits our ability to functionally map the disassembly of transcriptional and architectural complexes during processes like stress response or drug treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that comparative proximity labeling with a TurboID-CTCF fusion, followed by quantitative mass spectrometry, will identify proteins specifically evicted from or excluded from boundary loci upon a disruptive stimulus, revealing condition-specific mechanisms of insulator complex disassembly.
proposals.method: We will generate Drosophila S2 cell lines with an endogenously tagged CTCF-TurboID fusion protein. After validating localization, we will perform rapid (10-min) biotinylation pulses under control conditions and after treatment with aclarubicin, a drug known to stimulate Pol II elongation and potentially disrupt promoter-proximal boundaries. Biotinylated proteins will be streptavidin-purified and analyzed by label-free quantitative mass spectrometry (MaxQuant, Perseus). Statistical comparison will identify proteins with significantly reduced labeling in the drug-treated condition, representing 'eviction candidates'. Hits will be validated by ChIP-qPCR and live-cell imaging to confirm locus-specific loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel 'negative selection' method for mapping protein complex eviction from chromatin in vivo. It will provide the first functional map of proteins dynamically lost from CTCF boundaries upon transcriptional perturbation, linking drug-induced Pol II elongation changes to insulator stability. The approach will be broadly applicable to studying complex disassembly at any targetable genomic locus.
proposals.novelty: While proximity labeling is used to identify associated factors, its application as a comparative tool to specifically detect protein loss or eviction from a defined chromatin locus is novel. This inverts the standard logic of interactome capture, analogous to comparative virology screens for absent host factors, to reveal condition-specific complex disassembly.

proposals.title: Mapping the Evicted Interactome: Profiling DNA-Free CTCF and Cohesin Complexes upon Transcriptional Disruption at Chromatin Boundaries
proposals.motivation: CTCF and cohesin are essential for establishing chromatin boundaries and topologically associating domains (TADs), but their dynamic regulation remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 work, including Henikoff's on RNA-mediated CTCF binding, suggests transcription and specific drugs like aclarubicin can alter polymerase dynamics and potentially disrupt these complexes. However, existing methods map proteins bound to DNA, missing the critical population evicted from chromatin, which is key to understanding boundary plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific perturbations (e.g., aclarubicin-induced promoter-proximal polymerase elongation or targeted degradation of boundary-associated RNAs) will cause the eviction of a specific subset of CTCF/cohesin complexes from chromatin, and that these DNA-free complexes possess a distinct protein composition reflective of their functional state.
proposals.method: We will adapt and integrate established pre-2026 techniques. First, we will use biotinylated dCas9-APEX2 targeted to specific boundary loci in Drosophila or mammalian cells for proximity-dependent biotinylation of resident proteins. Following perturbation, we will perform sequential fractionation to isolate the soluble nuclear fraction, then use streptavidin capture to purify proteins that were chromatin-bound prior to perturbation but are now evicted. These 'evicted interactomes' will be identified via quantitative mass spectrometry (SILAC) and compared to stable chromatin-bound complexes. Data will be analyzed using algorithms like SAINT for specific interaction scoring and integrated with existing ChIP-seq and PRO-seq maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of condition-specific, DNA-free architectural protein complexes, directly testing models of RNA-mediated insulation. It will establish a novel 'eviction profiling' methodology to study complex dynamics beyond chromatin binding. The findings will reveal how transcriptional perturbations reshape the functional pool of boundary factors, linking polymerase activity to domain stability.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard paradigm of mapping protein-DNA interactions to focus on the evicted fraction, a functionally important but invisible population in assays like ChIP. It uniquely combines targeted chromatin labeling with post-perturbation soluble proteome capture to trace the fate of specific complexes.

proposals.title: Regulatory Genome Expansion as a Driver of Metazoan Complexity: A Comparative Analysis of Chromatin Boundary and Promoter Architecture
proposals.motivation: The evolution of phenotypic complexity in multicellular organisms has been historically attributed to the expansion of protein-coding gene families. However, the vast non-coding regulatory genome, which orchestrates precise spatiotemporal gene expression, remains underexplored as a primary evolutionary driver. This project directly tests the hypothesis that the elaboration of regulatory mechanisms, particularly chromatin domain insulation and promoter complexity, is a fundamental source of innovation distinguishing metazoans from simpler eukaryotes like yeast.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the acquisition and functional diversification of regulatory elements—specifically, the CTCF/cohesin-mediated boundary architecture and the proliferation of closely spaced, divergent promoters—underpin the increased regulatory capacity and phenotypic complexity of metazoans, independent of protein domain expansion.
proposals.method: We will perform a comparative genomic and epigenomic analysis between Drosophila melanogaster and Saccharomyces cerevisiae. Using existing and newly generated CUT&RUN data for CTCF, cohesin, and RNA polymerase II (Pol II) with specific phospho-Ser5 and Ser2 antibodies, we will map boundary elements and promoter-proximal pausing landscapes. We will integrate these maps with nascent transcription data (PRO-seq) and histone modification profiles (e.g., H3K4me3, H3K27ac) to define functional regulatory units. A key analysis will involve using computational models to quantify the density and strength of boundaries relative to gene clusters and to correlate divergent promoter architecture with Pol II elongation dynamics, inspired by aclarubicin-stimulation studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic framework linking specific non-coding genomic features—boundary insulation and promoter architecture—to the evolution of gene regulatory networks. It will shift the narrative from protein-centric to regulation-centric explanations of complexity and establish a comparative paradigm for studying regulatory genome evolution.
proposals.novelty: While boundary elements and promoter types are studied individually, this proposal uniquely integrates them into a unified model of regulatory genome expansion and directly tests their combined contribution to metazoan innovation through a focused yeast-to-fly comparison, inverting the traditional view of the non-coding genome as secondary.

proposals.title: Chromatin domain boundary evolution and the assembly of complex signaling loci from yeast to metazoans
proposals.motivation: The evolution of complex metazoan signaling pathways required the coordinated assembly of multiple protein domains and regulatory elements. Pre-2026 work shows that CTCF-mediated chromatin boundaries are crucial for insulating developmental gene regulation in animals, but their role in the evolutionary assembly of complex loci is unknown. This project investigates how the emergence of specific chromatin architectures facilitated the genomic integration of new domains and enhancers to form sophisticated signaling hubs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the evolutionary acquisition and reinforcement of CTCF-bound chromatin domain boundaries were prerequisite events that enabled the stable genomic assembly and coordinated regulation of complex multi-domain signaling proteins.
proposals.method: We will perform comparative CUT&RUN and nascent transcription profiling (PRO-seq) across species from yeast to Drosophila to map CTCF occupancy, histone modifications, and promoter-proximal polymerase pausing at orthologous signaling loci. Using phylogenetic shadowing and motif analysis, we will trace the evolutionary insertion of boundary elements relative to domain accretion events. In Drosophila, we will test boundary necessity using CRISPR/Cas9-mediated CTCF motif disruption at specific loci and assay changes in ectopic enhancer-promoter contacts and transcriptional interference using 3C-qPCR and PRO-seq.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between the evolution of chromatin domain insulation and the genomic innovation of complex signaling pathways. It will reveal whether boundary formation preceded or followed domain assembly, providing a mechanistic model for metazoan genome complexity. The findings will bridge evolutionary genomics with chromatin biology.
proposals.novelty: While CTCF's role in insulation is known, its evolutionary co-option as a facilitator of domain accretion in signaling loci is unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates phylogenetic analysis of chromatin states with functional dissection of boundaries in a model genetic system.

proposals.title: Synthetic Minimal Eukaryotic Chromosomes: Defining the Core Chromatin Architecture for Genome Stability and Transcription
proposals.motivation: Synthesizing minimal genomes is a powerful approach to understand the essential principles of life. While bacterial and yeast minimal genomes have been explored, a systematic, cross-eukaryotic design based on conserved core genes from yeast, fly, and worm is lacking. This project will define the minimal chromatin and regulatory architecture required for stable eukaryotic genome function, directly testing the necessity of features like boundaries and insulators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal synthetic eukaryotic genome, constructed from evolutionarily conserved core genes, will require a specific, sparse arrangement of CTCF binding sites and promoter architecture to maintain stable transcription and prevent aberrant enhancer-promoter interactions.
proposals.method: We will design minimal chromosomes by integrating conserved gene sets from S. cerevisiae, D. melanogaster, and C. elegans using comparative genomics tools like OrthoFinder. Chromatin architecture will be engineered by mapping conserved non-coding sequences and computationally predicting minimal CTCF site placement using existing algorithms (e.g., CTCFBSDB 2.0, motif scanning). We will synthesize these designs in yeast using established SCRaMbLE and transformation-associated recombination (TAR) methods, assessing viability, chromosome conformation (via Hi-C), and transcription (via precision run-on sequencing, PRO-seq) to evaluate boundary function and promoter activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first functionally tested blueprint for a cross-eukaryotic minimal genome, identifying the essential non-coding architectural elements. It will directly test how CTCF sites and promoter spacing, informed by Henikoff's work on boundaries and polymerase dynamics, contribute to insulation in a radically simplified context.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond single-species minimal genomes to a synthetic eukaryotic core, and in explicitly engineering and testing the necessity of chromatin architectural features, like CTCF-mediated boundaries, in this de novo context, which has not been done.

proposals.title: Defining the Chromatin Assembly Machinery for Replication-Independent H3.3 Deposition at CTCF Boundaries and Active Enhancers
proposals.motivation: The histone variant H3.3 is deposited in a replication-independent (RI) manner at regulatory elements like promoters, enhancers, and chromatin domain boundaries, playing a critical role in epigenetic memory and transcription. While the HIRA complex is known for RI deposition at promoters, the specific chaperone machinery responsible for H3.3 deposition at other key genomic loci, particularly CTCF-bound boundaries and active enhancers, remains poorly defined in metazoans. Identifying these factors is essential to understand how chromatin states are dynamically maintained outside of S-phase to regulate insulation and enhancer function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, non-HIRA histone chaperone complexes are responsible for the targeted, replication-independent deposition of H3.3 at CTCF-mediated chromatin boundaries and at active enhancers, and that this deposition is functionally linked to boundary strength and enhancer-promoter communication.
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted proteomics approach in Drosophila S2 cells, using endogenously tagged H3.3 for affinity purification coupled with mass spectrometry (AP-MS) under conditions that inhibit replication. To identify chaperones specific to boundaries and enhancers, we will perform chromatin immunoprecipitation followed by sequencing (ChIP-seq) for candidate chaperones and H3.3 in wild-type and factor-depleted cells. We will integrate these datasets with existing maps of CTCF binding, chromatin domains (TADs), and enhancer RNAs (eRNAs), leveraging computational tools like SICER and HOMER for peak calling and motif analysis. Functional validation will involve RNAi knockdown of candidate chaperones, followed by assays for boundary insulation (using published Hi-C methods), enhancer activity (e.g., STARR-seq or reporter assays), and Pol II elongation dynamics at divergent promoters, as in our prior aclarubicin work.
