proposals.contribution: This work will define the specific histone chaperone machinery for RI H3.3 deposition at architectural and regulatory elements, filling a major gap in chromatin biology. It will establish a direct mechanistic link between histone variant dynamics, chromatin architecture, and transcriptional regulation, explaining how epigenetic information is maintained at loci critical for genome organization.
proposals.novelty: While HIRA's role is established, this proposal seeks the unknown chaperones for H3.3 at boundaries and enhancers, integrating Henikoff's expertise in chromatin dynamics, CTCF, and promoter-proximal regulation to solve a defined, unsolved problem in the field.

proposals.title: Genome-wide mapping of H3.3 deposition dynamics reveals its role in CTCF-mediated insulation and promoter-proximal escape
proposals.motivation: The replication-independent histone variant H3.3 is a critical marker of active chromatin, deposited at promoters, enhancers, and regulatory elements. However, a comprehensive, high-resolution map of all H3.3 deposition sites across diverse cell types is lacking, limiting our understanding of its precise role in organizing functional chromatin domains and regulating transcription. Given the Henikoff lab's focus on chromatin boundaries and promoter escape, defining the full H3.3 landscape is essential to test its interplay with key architectural proteins like CTCF and RNA Polymerase II dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that H3.3 deposition is a universal mark of all active regulatory elements and that its occupancy at chromatin domain boundaries, particularly in conjunction with CTCF and RNA-mediated interactions, is crucial for strengthening insulation and facilitating promoter-proximal Pol II escape.
proposals.method: We will employ the high-resolution, low-input CUT&RUN technique, optimized by the Henikoff lab, with an antibody specific for the H3.3 N-terminal tail, across multiple Drosophila and mammalian cell lines, including male germline cells. This will be integrated with existing datasets for CTCF, Pol II (including its elongation state), and nascent RNA. Data analysis will utilize established pipelines for CUT&RUN peak calling (SEACR) and will be correlated with chromatin domain boundaries defined by Hi-C and with promoter-proximal pausing metrics from PRO-seq or NET-seq data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, high-resolution atlas of H3.3 deposition, directly linking its occupancy to the functional state of promoters, enhancers, and insulators. It will mechanistically test how H3.3 cooperates with CTCF to establish boundaries and how its presence at promoters influences the release of paused Pol II, integrating epigenomic marking with transcriptional dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While H3.3 is known to mark active genes, its systematic mapping in conjunction with architectural proteins and elongation machinery is novel. This proposal uniquely connects H3.3 deposition directly to the lab's recent findings on RNA-mediated CTCF binding at boundaries and small molecule-induced changes in Pol II escape, moving beyond correlation to test functional interdependence.

proposals.title: H3.3 deposition directs chromatin loop architecture at active enhancers and promoters
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work shows the histone variant H3.3 marks active regulatory elements like promoters and enhancers, and its deposition is linked to transcription. However, its role in shaping the 3D genome architecture, which is crucial for enhancer-promoter communication and insulation, is poorly understood. This gap is significant because understanding how histone variant dynamics influence chromatin folding is key to deciphering transcriptional regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that H3.3 deposition, particularly at active enhancers and promoters, is a key driver for the formation or stabilization of specific chromatin loops, and that this function is mediated through recruitment of architectural proteins like CTCF and RNA polymerase II.
proposals.method: We will perform H3.3 ChIP-seq and Hi-C (or promoter capture Hi-C) in Drosophila S2 cells and male germline cells, leveraging Henikoff's expertise in these systems. To test causality, we will use auxin-inducible degron systems to rapidly deplete HIRA or DAXX, the H3.3 deposition complexes, and perform time-course Hi-C to assess changes in loop and domain boundaries. Data analysis will involve integrating H3.3 peaks with Hi-C contact maps, CTCF/cohesin ChIP-seq, and RNA-seq, using established tools like HiC-Pro and Juicer.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link histone variant turnover to the dynamic 3D genome, revealing a mechanistic role for H3.3 beyond marking active chromatin. It will test whether H3.3 facilitates enhancer-promoter looping or strengthens boundary insulation, providing a unifying model for how transcription and chromatin architecture are coupled.
proposals.novelty: While H3.3 is known to mark active loci and CTCF/RNAPII are known to mediate looping, directly testing if H3.3 deposition is a prerequisite for specific chromatin conformations is novel. This moves beyond correlation to establish a causal, mechanistic link between histone variant dynamics and 3D genome organization.

proposals.title: H3.3 incorporation as a mechanism for heterochromatic boundary erosion and transcriptional escape in Drosophila pericentromeres
proposals.motivation: Heterochromatin, particularly at large repetitive regions like pericentromeres, is essential for genome stability but can aberrantly silence nearby genes. The histone variant H3.3 is incorporated in a replication-independent manner at active loci and is implicated in counteracting silencing, yet its specific role in destabilizing established heterochromatin at repetitive domains remains unclear. This is critical for understanding how heterochromatic boundaries are maintained and how inappropriate gene expression, or 'escape,' can occur.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted incorporation of H3.3 into constitutive heterochromatin, particularly at pericentromeric regions, disrupts the local chromatin environment by interfering with H3K9me2/3 propagation and HP1 binding, thereby weakening the insulating boundary and promoting transcriptional escape of embedded or proximal genes.
proposals.method: We will use the Drosophila model system, leveraging existing H3.3 mutants and a targeted dCas9-TET/ALKBH system fused to H3.3 to direct its incorporation to specific repetitive sequences (e.g., 359-bp satellite) in the male germline or somatic cells. We will assess changes using CUT&RUN for H3K9me3, HP1, and Pol II occupancy, alongside nascent RNA-seq to detect escape transcription. We will integrate these datasets with existing CTCF/cohesin maps to determine if H3.3-induced changes correlate with boundary weakening, using established insulation score algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a mechanistic link between a specific histone variant and the stability of heterochromatic silencing at repetitive DNA. It will define whether H3.3 acts as an endogenous 'anti-silencing' factor that can erode heterochromatic boundaries, providing a new model for how epigenetic states at these challenging regions are dynamically regulated.
proposals.novelty: While H3.3 is known to mark active chromatin, its targeted role in actively disrupting pre-existing, large-scale heterochromatin, rather than passively occupying already active regions, is a non-obvious and untested function. This proposal uniquely combines Henikoff's expertise in chromatin dynamics at boundaries with a direct perturbation of histone variant deposition.

proposals.title: Histone H3 eviction and variant replacement as a mechanism for stable transcriptional silencing at insulated chromatin boundaries
proposals.motivation: While histone deposition and variant incorporation are well-studied in active transcription, the chromatin-based mechanisms for establishing and maintaining stable silencing are less clear. Given Henikoff's work on CTCF boundaries and promoter-proximal polymerase dynamics, understanding how silencing is actively enforced at boundaries is crucial for a complete model of domain regulation. This addresses a key gap in the pre-2026 understanding of how insulator elements function not just as passive barriers but as active organizers of repressive chromatin.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted eviction of canonical H3 and its replacement with a specific histone variant (e.g., H3.3) is a required, active step for establishing stable transcriptional silencing at CTCF-bound chromatin domain boundaries. This process is distinct from and complementary to the well-characterized H3.3 deposition at active enhancers and promoters.
proposals.method: We will use Drosophila models and CUT&RUN in germline and somatic tissues to map histone H3 occupancy and variants (H3.3, cenH3) with nucleotide resolution at boundaries defined by CTCF/cohesin. We will integrate this with PRO-seq data to correlate polymerase pausing/elongation changes with histone dynamics. To test causality, we will employ auxin-inducible degradation of CTCF and use aclarubicin treatment (as in Henikoff's recent work) to perturb polymerase elongation, measuring subsequent changes in H3 occupancy via time-resolved CUT&RUN to distinguish eviction from replacement.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a specific chromatin remodeling event—histone variant exchange—as a core mechanism for boundary-mediated silencing. It will directly link polymerase elongation states to nucleosome stability at insulators. The findings will provide a mechanistic counterpart to models of active insulation, unifying concepts of transcription, chromatin dynamics, and genome architecture.
proposals.novelty: The proposal inverts the dominant paradigm focusing on histone deposition for activation, instead investigating eviction/replacement for silencing. It uniquely connects Henikoff's expertise in polymerase dynamics and boundary elements to the under-explored question of how nucleosome turnover directly enforces transcriptional repression.

proposals.title: Cell-type-specific patterns of replication-independent H3.3 deposition define tissue-specific chromatin landscapes and insulator function
proposals.motivation: The histone variant H3.3, deposited in a replication-independent (RI) manner, is a key marker of active chromatin and regulatory elements. While its role in transcription and memory is established, it is unknown whether the genomic targeting and functional consequences of RI H3.3 deposition vary systematically across cell types. Understanding this variation is crucial for linking chromatin dynamics to cell identity and the tissue-specific function of insulators and boundaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cell-type-specific transcription factors and enhancer activity direct RI H3.3 deposition to distinct genomic loci, including CTCF-bound boundaries, and that these deposition patterns are a major determinant of tissue-specific chromatin domain architecture and gene expression.
proposals.method: We will perform CUT&RUN for H3.3, H3K4me3, H3K27ac, and CTCF in a panel of Drosophila cell types (e.g., S2, ML-DmBG3, embryonic neurons) and mouse embryonic stem cells versus differentiated lineages. We will integrate these maps with nascent transcription (PRO-seq) and chromatin accessibility (ATAC-seq) data. Computational analysis will use the SICER2 algorithm to identify differential H3.3 domains and correlate them with cell-type-specific changes in insulation scores, promoter-enhancer interactions (from published Hi-C), and RNA polymerase II elongation dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a comprehensive map of cell-type-specific RI chromatin dynamics, directly testing if H3.3 deposition patterns are a tissue-specific feature of genome organization. It will reveal how variation in histone variant dynamics at boundaries contributes to cell-type-specific gene regulation and chromatin domain insulation.
proposals.novelty: While H3.3 is known to mark active elements, a systematic, cross-tissue comparison of its deposition dynamics and its functional interplay with CTCF-mediated insulation has not been performed. This moves beyond cataloging static marks to understanding the cell-type-specific processes that shape the epigenome.

proposals.title: Regulatory Element-Centric Genome Annotation Reveals Novel Principles of CTCF-Mediated Insulation and Promoter-Enhancer Communication
proposals.motivation: Current genome annotation is fundamentally biased toward protein-coding genes, treating regulatory elements like enhancers and boundaries as secondary annotations. This protein-centric view limits our understanding of genome organization and regulation, particularly for mechanisms like CTCF-mediated insulation and enhancer-promoter specificity. A regulatory element-first framework is needed to systematically uncover how these features collectively orchestrate transcription.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that re-annotating the genome from the perspective of core regulatory elements—specifically promoters, enhancers, and CTCF/cohesin-bound boundaries—will reveal novel, conserved architectural rules governing transcription and chromatin domain formation that are obscured by gene-centric models.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution maps of regulatory elements (CUT&RUN for CTCF, cohesin, histone modifications) and nascent transcription (PRO-seq) from Drosophila tissues into a unified annotation. Using graph-based algorithms, we will model the genome as a network of regulatory nodes (elements) connected by looping (Hi-C) or co-activity (transcription). We will specifically test how divergent promoter architecture and RNA-mediated CTCF binding, as suggested by Henikoff's recent work, influence network connectivity and insulation strength in this element-centric model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, publicly available regulatory element-centric genome annotation for a model organism. It will establish a generalizable computational and conceptual framework for understanding genome regulation from a noncoding perspective and yield specific mechanistic insights into how promoter directionality and RNA polymerase dynamics contribute to boundary function.
proposals.novelty: While existing studies examine individual regulatory elements, this proposal inverts the foundational logic of genome annotation itself, making noncoding elements the primary units of analysis. This shift enables the discovery of system-level organizational principles that are invisible when regulatory features are viewed merely as accessories to genes.

proposals.title: Cell-type-specific dynamics of chromatin domain architecture revealed by CTCF boundary plasticity and promoter-driven insulation
proposals.motivation: Chromatin domains, defined by CTCF/cohesin loops and topologically associating domains (TADs), are typically mapped as static, population-averaged features. However, pre-2026 studies, including Henikoff's work on germline-specific maturation and RNA-mediated CTCF binding, suggest these structures are cell-type-specific and may be dynamically regulated. The functional consequence of this plasticity for gene regulation, especially for genes at domain 'arms' versus stable 'centers', remains poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chromatin domain definitions are dynamic and cell-type-specific, with 'boundary strength' and internal architecture modulated by local transcriptional activity and RNA polymerase II occupancy, leading to cell-type-specific patterns of gene insulation and enhancer-promoter communication.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution, cell-type-specific chromatin interaction maps (Micro-C/Hi-C) from Drosophila tissues with matched CUT&RUN profiles for CTCF, cohesin, and histone modifications (H3K27ac, H3K27me3). We will develop a novel computational pipeline, 'DynaDom', that uses a hidden Markov model to segment chromosomes into probabilistic domain states rather than fixed boundaries, incorporating RNA polymerase II ChIP-seq and nascent transcription (PRO-seq) data as covariates. This will allow us to classify loci as stable domain 'cores' or dynamic 'arms' and test their association with cell-type-specific gene expression and enhancer activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for modeling chromatin architecture as a probabilistic, cell-state-specific feature rather than a fixed map. It will directly test how transcription and promoter activity, as shown in Henikoff's aclarubicin study, contribute to boundary establishment and insulation, moving the field beyond descriptive cataloguing towards a mechanistic understanding of domain plasticity.
proposals.novelty: While TADs are known to vary, our approach uniquely models this variation as a continuous property integrated with real-time transcriptional output, challenging the canonical view of CTCF-anchored loops as the sole determinant of static domain structure.

proposals.title: Cooperative Kinetics, Not Just CTCF Binding, Determine Boundary Strength and Enhancer-Promoter Communication
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide maps of transcription factor (TF) binding, such as for the insulator protein CTCF, are foundational to predicting chromatin architecture and gene expression. However, recent work from the Henikoff lab shows that active enhancers can strengthen CTCF-mediated insulation via RNA interactions, and that drug-induced changes in RNA polymerase II elongation kinetics can alter promoter output. This suggests that static binding maps may be insufficient to predict functional outcomes, as they ignore the dynamic, cooperative kinetics of the transcription machinery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional sufficiency of architectural protein binding sites (e.g., CTCF boundaries) to insulate or facilitate enhancer-promoter communication is determined not by occupancy alone, but by the cooperative kinetics of RNA polymerase II initiation, elongation, and termination at closely spaced regulatory elements.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution genomic data from Drosophila models with kinetic modeling. Using CUT&RUN for CTCF and cohesin binding, PRO-seq for engaged polymerase positions, and nascent RNA-seq, we will quantify binding and transcription at defined boundaries and divergent promoters. We will perturb kinetics using small molecules like aclarubicin, which affects Pol II elongation. A computational model will be built, extending concepts from thermodynamic models of TF binding to include kinetic parameters of polymerase progression and co-transcriptional RNA-DNA interactions, to predict expression changes from binding data under different kinetic regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework that integrates static chromatin architecture data with the dynamic kinetics of transcription to predict gene expression. It will directly test the limits of binding-based predictive models and establish a new, kinetically informed understanding of boundary element function and enhancer specificity.
proposals.novelty: While many studies correlate TF binding with expression, this proposal uniquely posits that the kinetic interplay between Pol II at promoters and RNA-mediated stabilization at boundaries is a critical, overlooked variable determining the functional output of a binding site.

proposals.title: Transcription and Splicing as Drivers of CTCF-Mediated Chromatin Boundary Formation in Drosophila
proposals.motivation: Chromatin organization, particularly the establishment of boundaries and topologically associating domains (TADs) by CTCF, is widely considered a primary determinant of gene expression. However, recent pre-2026 work, including Henikoff's own findings on RNA-mediated CTCF binding and polymerase elongation, suggests transcription itself may actively shape chromatin architecture. This proposal seeks to directly test this inverted causality, a critical unresolved question in genome regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that active RNA polymerase II transcription and co-transcriptional splicing generate RNA-protein complexes that recruit and stabilize CTCF at specific loci, thereby reinforcing or establishing new chromatin boundaries in a transcription-dependent manner.
proposals.method: We will use Drosophila models, leveraging Henikoff's expertise in germline epigenomics. We will employ targeted degradation of RNA polymerase II (using the auxin-inducible degron system) and pharmacological inhibition of splicing (with Pladienolide B) in a tissue-specific manner. Subsequent changes in CTCF occupancy, chromatin accessibility (using CUT&RUN), and 3D chromatin architecture (using Micro-C) will be mapped. We will specifically analyze loci with closely spaced divergent promoters, known to be sensitive to elongation dynamics, to test for boundary loss or gain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct experimental evidence for transcription and splicing as active, causal agents in chromatin domain organization. It will define a mechanistic pathway from RNA synthesis to CTCF stabilization and boundary formation, moving beyond correlative observations. The findings will fundamentally shift the understanding of the gene expression-chromatin relationship from a static hierarchy to a dynamic, reciprocal loop.
proposals.novelty: While CTCF's role in structuring chromatin is well-established, the idea that its localization is a consequence, not solely a cause, of transcription at specific loci is non-obvious. This proposal uniquely combines acute transcriptional/splicing perturbation with high-resolution chromatin topology assays to test this direction of causality directly, building on Henikoff's recent insights into RNA-mediated CTCF binding.

proposals.title: Functional validation of modENCODE-predicted noncoding RNAs in Drosophila chromatin domain insulation and promoter regulation
proposals.motivation: The modENCODE project generated a comprehensive annotation of noncoding RNAs (ncRNAs) in Drosophila, but the biological functions of most remain unvalidated. Given the established roles of ncRNAs in chromatin organization and gene regulation, particularly through mechanisms like RNA-mediated CTCF binding at boundaries, systematic functional testing is critical to move from prediction to mechanistic understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of modENCODE-predicted ncRNAs function as architectural elements, contributing to chromatin domain insulation via interactions with CTCF or by modulating RNA polymerase II dynamics at promoters, thereby influencing transcriptional output.
proposals.method: We will perform targeted RNAi knockdown of high-confidence candidate ncRNAs in Drosophila S2 cells, selected based on their genomic proximity to CTCF sites or divergent promoters. Functional impact on insulation will be assayed by 4C-seq and Hi-C to detect changes in chromatin looping and boundary strength. Effects on transcription will be measured by precision run-on sequencing (PRO-seq) to assess RNA polymerase II promoter-proximal pausing and elongation, building on methods for analyzing closely spaced divergent promoters. Candidate ncRNA-protein interactions will be probed by CLIP-seq for CTCF and Pol II.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct experimental evidence for the roles of specific ncRNAs in 3D genome organization and transcriptional regulation, bridging the gap between modENCODE annotations and functional genomics. It will establish a validated set of functional ncRNAs and clarify mechanisms of RNA-mediated boundary activity and promoter control.
proposals.novelty: While RNA-mediated CTCF binding is known, systematically testing modENCODE ncRNA candidates for this function is novel. Furthermore, integrating insulation assays with direct measurements of Pol II dynamics following ncRNA loss-of-function provides a unique, multi-mechanism validation strategy.

proposals.title: Integrated Hierarchical Networks Reveal Post-Transcriptional Modulation of Chromatin Domain Boundary Strength
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including Henikoff's own, shows that chromatin domain boundaries, enforced by CTCF and cohesin, are dynamically regulated by transcription and non-coding RNAs. However, hierarchical gene regulatory networks primarily map transcription factor and microRNA interactions, neglecting a critical layer: RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) that control mRNA stability and translation. This gap limits our understanding of how boundary-associated genes and their regulatory RNAs are themselves post-transcriptionally controlled to fine-tune boundary integrity and transcriptional escape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RNA-binding proteins form a post-transcriptional regulatory layer that targets mRNAs encoding boundary-defining factors (e.g., CTCF, cohesin subunits, pioneer factors), and that this layer is essential for the dynamic maturation and stabilization of chromatin architecture during cellular differentiation.
proposals.method: We will integrate existing and new CLIP-seq datasets for major RBPs (e.g., ELAVL1, IGF2BP) with chromatin interaction maps (Hi-C), CTCF/cohesin ChIP-seq, and RNA-seq from a Drosophila germline differentiation time-course. A custom computational pipeline will extend hierarchical network inference algorithms (e.g., based on GENIE3 or PANDA) to incorporate RBP-mRNA interactions and inferred mRNA stability changes. This integrated network will be analyzed for motifs where specific transcription factors, boundary-associated enhancer RNAs, and RBPs converge to regulate key architectural protein mRNAs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first multi-layered regulatory network linking transcriptional and post-transcriptional control to chromatin architecture. It will identify specific RBPs and mRNA stability programs that modulate the dosage of boundary components, providing a mechanistic explanation for the observed plasticity of insulation during germline epigenome maturation.
proposals.novelty: While studies have linked transcription to boundary function, the direct integration of post-transcriptional regulatory networks into models of 3D genome dynamics is unexplored. This moves beyond correlative analysis to model how RBP activity could actively buffer or potentiate changes in boundary strength defined by transcriptional regulators.

proposals.title: Transcription Factor Hotspots as Chromatin Insulators and Promoters of Transcriptional Escape in Drosophila
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide mapping has identified loci bound by an unusually high number of transcription factors (TFs), termed 'TF hotspots', but their functional significance remains unclear. Given the Henikoff lab's focus on chromatin boundaries, CTCF, and promoter-proximal regulation, we hypothesize these hotspots are critical for organizing chromatin domains and modulating promoter escape, a key regulatory step. Understanding their role will bridge the gap between TF binding landscapes and higher-order genome architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: TF hotspots function as composite regulatory elements that both insulate chromatin domains via cooperative CTCF/cohesin binding and facilitate transcriptional escape from promoter-proximal pausing at embedded or adjacent divergent promoters.
proposals.method: We will map TF hotspots in Drosophila S2 cells using existing ChIP-seq compendia and define their relationship to CTCF/cohesin sites, sub-topologically associating domain (sub-TAD) boundaries, and divergent promoters. Using CUT&RUN, we will profile Pol II Ser5p and Ser2p phosphorylation to assess promoter-proximal pausing and escape efficiency at hotspot-associated promoters. We will employ auxin-inducible degradation of CTCF and cohesin subunit Rad21, combined with Micro-C and nascent transcriptomics (PRO-seq), to test the direct role of hotspots in insulation and promoter escape. Small molecule perturbation of elongation (e.g., aclarubicin) will probe the functional coupling between insulation and transcription.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct mechanistic link between high-density TF occupancy, chromatin domain insulation, and the regulation of Pol II elongation. It will determine whether TF hotspots are privileged sites for integrating enhancer signals and organizing local chromatin structure, providing a new model for composite regulatory elements.
proposals.novelty: While TF hotspots are known, their potential dual function as insulators and facilitators of promoter escape is unexplored. This integrates the lab's expertise in boundaries and promoter-proximal regulation to test a non-obvious, unified model for these enigmatic loci.

proposals.title: Causal Inference of Chromatin-Mediated Gene Regulation Using CTCF Boundary Perturbations and RNA Polymerase Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Chromatin marks and gene expression are strongly correlated, but correlation does not imply causation. Pre-2026 studies, including Henikoff's work on CTCF boundaries and enhancer-promoter interactions, highlight the need to distinguish whether specific chromatin features actively regulate transcription or are merely consequences of it. Establishing causality is essential for understanding developmental programs and disease mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted perturbations of CTCF boundaries, combined with precise measurements of RNA polymerase II (Pol II) initiation and elongation, will reveal causal, directional relationships between chromatin insulation states and gene expression outcomes, moving beyond correlative observations.
proposals.method: We will integrate experimental perturbation with causal inference modeling. First, we will use Drosophila models (extending Henikoff's germline work) to perform CTCF boundary deletions and monitor changes via CUT&RUN for histone marks and PRO-seq for Pol II dynamics. Second, we will apply causal discovery algorithms, specifically PCMCI (Peter-Clark Momentary Conditional Independence) and variants of Structural Causal Models, to time-series and perturbation data to infer directed graphs of regulatory influence between chromatin features and transcriptional output.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for causally linking chromatin architecture to gene regulation, specifically testing the role of boundaries in gene escape and enhancer insulation. It will generate testable models of how Pol II elongation changes, as seen with drugs like aclarubicin, are influenced by upstream chromatin states.
proposals.novelty: While many studies map correlations, this proposal uniquely combines Henikoff's expertise in boundary elements and Pol II kinetics with formal causal inference methods from machine learning, aiming to disentangle cause and effect in chromatin biology.

proposals.title: Predicting promoter escape and transcriptional bursting from integrated chromatin conformation and sequence data using deep convolutional neural networks
proposals.motivation: The precise regulation of transcription initiation and promoter escape is a fundamental, unresolved problem in gene regulation, with implications for development and disease. While recent work, including from our lab, has shown that CTCF boundaries and enhancer-promoter interactions are modulated by RNA polymerase II dynamics and chromatin state, we lack predictive models that integrate local sequence features with higher-order chromatin architecture to forecast expression outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning model trained on high-resolution chromatin conformation data (e.g., Micro-C), histone modification maps, and underlying DNA sequence can accurately predict cell-type-specific transcriptional output, particularly the probability of promoter escape and the magnitude of transcriptional bursting.
proposals.method: We will train a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, inspired by image recognition models like ResNet, on integrated genomic 'images'. Input tensors will combine one-hot encoded DNA sequence with aligned tracks of CTCF ChIP-seq, H3K27ac, H3K4me3, and Micro-C contact frequencies across Drosophila spermatogenesis stages. The model will be trained to predict nascent RNA levels (PRO-seq) as the output. We will specifically engineer the architecture to learn features related to divergent promoter spacing and boundary element insulation, building on our recent mechanistic findings.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a predictive, quantitative framework for gene regulation that synthesizes sequence and 3D chromatin information. It will identify novel combinatorial rules governing promoter escape and insulation, providing testable hypotheses for experimental validation in germline development.
proposals.novelty: While others have used CNNs to predict chromatin features from sequence, applying this architecture to integrate *in vivo* chromatin conformation data with sequence to directly predict polymerase dynamics is a novel synthesis, directly leveraging our unique datasets on germline chromosome maturation and RNA-mediated CTCF binding.

proposals.title: Single-cell mapping of chromatin boundaries and transcription factor dynamics in C. elegans reveals cell-type-specific insulation and promoter-escape mechanisms
proposals.motivation: While population-level epigenomic studies have defined general principles of chromatin domain organization and transcription, they mask critical cell-to-cell heterogeneity. In C. elegans, with its invariant lineage and fully characterized cell types, the relationship between cell fate, CTCF/cohesin-mediated insulation, and promoter-proximal polymerase dynamics remains unresolved at the single-cell level. This gap limits our understanding of how precise transcriptional programs are established and maintained during development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cell-type-specific chromatin boundaries, defined by CTCF occupancy and RNA-mediated interactions, enforce distinct transcriptional domains by modulating RNA Polymerase II escape from closely spaced divergent promoters.
proposals.method: We will adapt CUT&Tag for single-cell resolution (scCUT&Tag) in fixed C. elegans nuclei to map histone modifications (H3K27ac, H3K27me3) and CTCF binding. Parallelly, we will perform scATAC-seq to assess chromatin accessibility. To capture transcriptional dynamics, we will employ a modified scRNA-seq protocol enriching for promoter-proximal paused polymerase, building on insights from aclarubicin studies. Data integration will use a custom computational pipeline leveraging the C. elegans cell atlas, employing graph-based clustering and regression models to correlate boundary strength, TF occupancy, and polymerase escape efficiency across cell types.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, cell-type-resolved map of chromatin state and insulator binding in C. elegans, directly testing models of RNA-mediated boundary reinforcement in vivo. It will establish a causal framework linking specific boundary elements to the regulation of polymerase escape and transcriptional output in defined developmental lineages.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond correlative population averages to test mechanistic models of insulation and transcription initiation in a complete, in vivo metazoan system with single-cell resolution, uniquely combining Henikoff's expertise in chromatin profiling and polymerase dynamics within a developmental context.

proposals.title: Inferring Chromatin Domain Boundaries and Insulator States from Single-Cell RNA-Seq Profiles Using a CTCF-Aware Neural Network
proposals.motivation: Current models of gene regulation primarily use chromatin features to predict expression, but vast single-cell RNA-seq datasets lack corresponding chromatin profiles. The inverse problem—predicting chromatin from expression—is underexplored but critical for interpreting expression data from dynamic systems like development or disease. This is especially relevant for understanding insulator function, as CTCF-mediated boundaries are central to domain organization but their activity is often inferred indirectly.
proposals.hypothesis: Gene expression patterns, particularly of divergent promoter pairs and genes flanking putative boundaries, contain sufficient information to predict the location and strength of chromatin domain boundaries and the associated binding of architectural proteins like CTCF.
proposals.method: We will train a deep neural network (e.g., a 1D convolutional or transformer architecture) on paired single-cell ATAC-seq/RNA-seq data from Drosophila and mammalian cell lines to learn the mapping from expression vectors to chromatin state vectors (e.g., CTCF ChIP-seq, H3K27ac, H3K27me3). The model will be explicitly constrained using knowledge of CTCF motif positioning and the relationship between promoter-proximal polymerase dynamics and boundary strength, inspired by Henikoff's work on divergent transcription and RNA-mediated CTCF binding. Validation will involve predicting boundaries in germline maturation stages from existing expression-only datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational tool to infer high-resolution chromatin architecture from ubiquitous expression data, enabling retrospective analysis of existing RNA-seq cohorts. It will generate testable predictions about how transcriptional changes at specific loci, like closely spaced divergent promoters, functionally relate to insulator strength and domain integrity.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate expression and chromatin, a direct, predictive model of boundaries from expression is novel. The approach uniquely integrates specific mechanistic insights—RNA polymerase II elongation dynamics and RNA-mediated CTCF stabilization—as architectural priors within the machine learning framework.

ideas.title: Invert the gene expression to chromatin relationship
ideas.description: Investigate how transcription and splicing actively shape chromatin organization, not just the reverse.
ideas.sourceTitle: Integrative Analysis of the Caenorhabditis elegans Genome by the modENCODE Project

ideas.title: Scale down to single-cell resolution epigenomics
ideas.description: Generate chromatin modification and transcription factor binding maps at the resolution of individual worm cells.
ideas.sourceTitle: Integrative Analysis of the Caenorhabditis elegans Genome by the modENCODE Project

ideas.title: Nuclease-Specific Endpoint Amplification (NSEA) for High-Resolution Deletion Mapping
ideas.description: This fused approach combines the use of an alternative nuclease (e.g., DNase I, Benzonase, or a sequence-specific engineered nuclease) with a PCR strategy designed to directly amplify the unique deletion endpoints it creates. By designing primers that anneal to the nuclease-cleaved ends or to common linker sequences ligated to them, the method bypasses the need for fragment cloning. This synergy allows for the rapid, multiplexed analysis of deletion patterns specific to that nuclease's activity, providing both a functional assay for its cleavage preferences and a direct route to sequence the spectrum of genomic rearrangements it induces.

ideas.title: Use a Programmable Molecular Scaffold for Multi-Enzyme Recruitment to DNA
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from synthetic biology—the use of programmable protein scaffolds to co-localize multiple enzymes—to the problem of sequence-specific chromatin profiling. In the original metabolic engineering context, scaffolds with modular interaction domains are used to recruit enzymes of a pathway, enhancing intermediate channeling and efficiency. Adapted here, a dCas9 protein is engineered as a central DNA-targeting scaffold. It is fused to a set of orthogonal peptide interaction domains. Various chromatin effectors (e.g., nucleases, epigenetic writers/erasers, fluorescent proteins) are tagged with the cognate binding peptides. By expressing these components with a target-specific guide RNA, multiple enzymes can be simultaneously recruited to the same genomic locus. This enables complex, multi-parameter chromatin profiling and manipulation—such as concurrent cleavage, labeling, and modification—from a single, sequence-defined targeting event, moving beyond the one-enzyme-per-dCas9 limitation.

ideas.title: Differential Eviction Proteomics to Map Condition-Specific, DNA-Dissociated Protein Complexes
ideas.description: This approach maps protein complexes specifically evicted from DNA by employing a paired, differential methodology centered on a precise eviction trigger. It compares the composition of affinity-purified complexes under DNA-bound versus induced DNA-dissociated conditions, using quantitative mass spectrometry. This differential filter distinguishes genuine, condition-specific evictees from background complexes that are never DNA-associated. The result is a high-confidence map of functional protein assemblies whose stability, composition, or interactions are directly regulated by their dissociation from chromatin.

ideas.title: Chromatin Eviction Capture via Negative Selection and Proximity Labeling
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard target to capture protein complexes evicted from DNA. Drawing an analogy from virology's use of comparative interactomics to identify host factors absent in non-permissive cells, the adapted solution is to use comparative proximity labeling. By applying a rapid biotinylating enzyme (e.g., TurboID) fused to a DNA-binding protein at a specific locus, one can compare the proximitomes under two conditions (e.g., +/- drug, +/- stress). Mass spectrometry analysis of the biotinylated proteins, followed by quantitative comparison, will reveal proteins whose proximity labeling is significantly diminished in the test condition. These 'negative hits' represent the DNA-free complexes—either evicted, never recruited, or structurally rearranged to be out of range—providing a functional map of condition-specific complex disassembly or exclusion from chromatin.

ideas.title: Targeted Nuclear Envelope Permeabilization for In-Cell CUT&RUN
ideas.description: This redesigned protocol overcomes the barrier of the cytoplasm in intact cells by employing a photoactivatable enzyme system for precise, on-demand access to the nucleus. A pA-MNase conjugate is engineered with a caged NLS and a nuclear export signal (NES), causing it to be retained in the cytoplasm after initial cell permeabilization. Following antibody binding to surface or cytoplasmic epitopes, a brief UV pulse uncages the NLS, driving rapid nuclear import of the enzyme complex. This method ensures efficient chromatin cleavage by minimizing the time for cytoplasmic entrapment and degradation, preserving cellular context while achieving the high efficiency of traditional nuclear CUT&RUN.

ideas.title: Adapt single-molecule imaging techniques from structural biology to map nuclease activity in live cells
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from structural biology, specifically time-resolved cryo-EM techniques used to trap and visualize intermediate states of dynamic macromolecular complexes. The analogous solution involves rapid freezing to capture transient structural snapshots at millisecond resolution. Adapted to the original research context, this involves using super-resolution microscopy not just for static observation, but to visualize the in situ progression of DNA cleavage events. By implementing a rapid, triggerable fixation system (e.g., light-activated crosslinking) or synchronizing nuclease activity with photoswitchable probes, one can kinetically trap the cleavage process at defined time points. This allows for sequential super-resolution imaging snapshots to map the spatial-temporal dynamics of DNA breaks on chromatin within the native nuclear environment, prior to any extraction and sequencing steps.

ideas.title: A Tn5-MNase fusion with a chemically inducible dimerization switch for controlled, sequential tagmentation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from chemical biology's use of chemically inducible dimerization (CID) to control protein interactions with temporal precision, this idea adapts that solution to the challenge of fusing MNase to Tn5 transposase. The analogous CID solution uses a small molecule (e.g., rapamycin) to trigger the dimerization of two separate protein domains, activating a process only upon demand. The adapted research idea is to engineer a system where the MNase and Tn5 transposase are expressed as separate proteins, each fused to one half of a CID pair (e.g., FRB and FKBP). This allows researchers to first perform MNase digestion of chromatin under optimized conditions, then add the dimerizer drug to rapidly recruit Tn5 specifically to the freshly generated DNA ends for synchronized tagmentation and library preparation in a controlled, two-step reaction.

ideas.title: Adapting DNA End-Protection from Telomere Biology to Vector Design
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a 4-base 3' overhang is necessary to protect a linearized vector from degradation. It draws an analogy from telomere biology, where chromosome ends are protected not by overhangs but by specialized protein complexes (like shelterin) and DNA structures (T-loops) that cap the terminus and prevent its recognition as a DNA break. The adapted research idea is to engineer a cloning vector with a terminal sequence that recruits a designed 'capping' protein or forms a protective secondary structure. This biological cap would shield the vector's ends from exonucleases, eliminating the dependency on a 3' overhang for protection and potentially expanding the repertoire of usable restriction sites and cloning strategies.

ideas.title: A unified single-molecule instrument for deletion-scaffolded and enzymatic re-sequencing
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea creates a hybrid sequencing platform that overcomes the destructive vs. parallelized workflow conflict. It physically integrates a non-destructive, real-time enzymatic sequencing module (e.g., using engineered helicase-polymerase complexes) into the same nanofluidic or optical apparatus used for the deletion method. The process begins with the deletion method to generate a complete, ultra-long scaffold read from a single DNA molecule. This scaffold then directs the platform to use the co-localized enzymatic system to perform targeted, in-situ re-sequencing of specific regions of interest, such as homopolymers or complex repeats, on the same immobilized molecule. This approach preserves the long-read advantage while adding a complementary, confirmatory sequencing modality to resolve ambiguities and boost accuracy.

ideas.title: A Multi-Modal, Calibrated Integration Framework for CUT&RUN and Native ChIP-seq
ideas.description: This idea proposes to systematically integrate CUT&RUN and native ChIP-seq data by adapting a solution from geophysics. In seismology, different types of seismic waves (P-waves and S-waves) are jointly inverted to create a unified, accurate model of Earth's interior, as each wave probes different physical properties. Similarly, CUT&RUN (sensitive to epitope accessibility in permeabilized nuclei) and native ChIP-seq (sensitive to stable, native protein-DNA complexes) provide complementary but biased views of chromatin architecture. The adapted research idea is to develop a computational 'joint inversion' framework that uses a shared statistical model to co-analyze both datasets. This framework would cross-calibrate the methodological biases, resolve discrepancies, and output a consensus, high-confidence map of protein-DNA interactions and chromatin states, providing a more comprehensive and robust picture than any single methodology.

ideas.title: Single-tube, PCR-based deletion mapping using a specialized polymerase to directly amplify and repair ExoIII-digested ends
ideas.description: This fused idea combines the endpoint-specific amplification concept of PCR with the challenge to the S1 nuclease step. The core proposal is to use a specialized DNA polymerase (or polymerase blend) with intrinsic 3'->5' exonuclease and strong strand-displacement or end-repair activity to directly act on ExoIII-generated termini. This polymerase would both 'repair' the heterogeneous ends to create blunt or amplifiable ends and subsequently serve as the enzyme for the subsequent PCR amplification using primers designed to target the deletion junctions. The synergy eliminates multiple purification and enzymatic steps, condensing the entire deletion mapping protocol into a single-tube, isothermal or thermal-cycling reaction, drastically increasing speed and reducing potential artifacts.

ideas.title: Transposon-Mediated, Overhang-Independent Vector Insertion for High-Resolution Sequencing
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to engineer a transposase complex (e.g., derived from Tn5) that incorporates a protective modification or co-factor, such as a bound nucleotide analog or a fused terminal-binding protein, to shield the vector-insert junction from exonuclease activity without relying on a 3' single-stranded overhang. This modified transposon system would be used to generate random, sequence-tagged insertions in a target genome or DNA sample. The protected insertion sites then serve as universal, nuclease-resistant priming points for high-throughput sequencing, directly coupling the mutagenesis and library preparation steps. This integrated approach bypasses traditional enzymatic end-polishing and adapter ligation, streamlining the workflow for insertion sequencing and potentially enabling novel applications in complex or damaged DNA samples where standard overhang-dependent cloning fails.

ideas.title: Controlled, Non-Processive DNA Digestion via a Chemically Gated Nuclease
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption that a uniform digestion rate is necessary for generating deletions, proposing instead a non-processive nuclease with controlled pauses. Drawing an analogy from heterogeneous catalysis, where catalyst 'poisons' are used to create a non-uniform surface and control reaction progression, this solution is adapted. Here, a non-processive nuclease (the catalyst) is controlled by a reversible, DNA-binding chemical inhibitor (the poison). By pulsing the concentration of this inhibitor, the nuclease's activity is gated, creating intentional pauses. This allows for the generation of a diverse set of deletion products from a single DNA substrate in a controlled, stepwise manner, directly relaxing the requirement for a uniform digestion rate.

ideas.title: Chromatin Immunoprecipitation by Selective Non-Target Retention (ChIP-SNTR)
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP) release step strategy by drawing an analogy from industrial mineral processing, specifically reverse flotation. In reverse flotation, the gangue (waste rock) is made hydrophilic to remain in the aqueous phase, while the valuable ore is made hydrophobic and removed. Adapted to chromatin biology, the method would develop a way to selectively degrade, cross-link, or otherwise render non-target chromatin insoluble and easily separable in situ—effectively creating a 'chromatin gangue' matrix. The soluble fraction, now highly enriched for the target chromatin, can then be gently collected, potentially improving specificity and yield by avoiding the challenges of eluting targets from solid-phase beads.

ideas.title: Chemically Induced, Two-Step Recruitment for Specific Tagmentation of MNase-Cleaved DNA
ideas.description: This redesigned approach separates DNA cleavage from tagmentation to eliminate background. First, MNase digestion is performed to generate cleaved ends, which are specifically ligated to a biotinylated adaptor. Next, a streptavidin-dead Tn5 fusion is bound to these adaptors, localizing the tagmentation machinery exclusively to cleavage sites. Finally, active tagmentation is triggered by adding rapamycin to induce dimerization with a second adaptor-loaded Tn5 subunit, ensuring precise, on-demand incorporation of sequencing adaptors only at the MNase-cut locations. This sequential, inducible control guarantees high specificity and a low background signal.

ideas.title: Probabilistic deconvolution of context-dependent cleavage background
ideas.description: This method moves beyond simple subtraction by constructing a generative model of non-specific cleavage that is conditioned on multiple observable contexts, including local nucleotide sequence, chromatin accessibility data, and regional signal from negative control experiments. The model is trained on a diverse set of control datasets to capture the heterogeneity of background signal. For a new experiment, it performs a Bayesian deconvolution to estimate, for each observed cleavage event, the probability it originated from the non-specific background process versus the specific reaction of interest. The final output is a high-resolution, probabilistic activity map that robustly identifies specific signal even in regions of high and variable background.

ideas.title: Bidirectional Progressive Deletion Mapping via Convergent Exonucleases
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that digestion is only useful in one direction by proposing a bidirectional deletion strategy for mapping, inspired by controlled polymer degradation in chemistry. The analogous solution from polymer chemistry uses complementary catalysts to degrade a polymer chain from both ends simultaneously, converging on stable central regions. Adapted to genomics, the research idea is to employ two orthogonal, processive exonucleases (e.g., one 5'→3' and one 3'→5') to digest a DNA template from both termini at once. The surviving fragments, which resist convergent digestion, would rapidly and precisely map the locations of bound proteins, stable structures, or other protective elements, as these would appear as 'islands' in a deletion landscape that converges from both ends.

ideas.title: Genetically encoded nickase-mediated fragmentation with unified adapter ligation for unbiased click-chemistry barcoding
ideas.description: This approach integrates a uniform, engineered DNA cassette into cellular genomes to serve as a universal starting point for fragmentation and barcoding. A sequence-agnostic nickase or transposase, pre-complexed with a sample-specific adapter containing a bioorthogonal click handle, simultaneously fragments at the cassette and ligates the adapter in a single, efficient step. This ensures every DNA fragment from a given sample receives an identical, covalently attached barcode adapter with minimal sequence-dependent bias. The samples are then pooled, and the uniform click handles enable robust, multiplexed conjugation to detection moieties or surfaces via bioorthogonal chemistry for downstream analysis.

Immediately after imaging, cells are fixed in situ on the imaging chamber, which is integrated with a microfluidic device that captures and barcodes nucleic acids while preserving spatial information from the imaging data. This enables direct, causal linkage between the observed molecular dynamics and the sequencing output from the identical cellular context.

High-throughput sequencing of the transposon-genome junctions then captures both the insertion site and its associated barcode. This allows for unambiguous identification and absolute quantification of each insertion mutant within complex pooled assays, directly addressing the mapping and quantification limitations of standard insertion sequencing.

ideas.title: Define the Cellular Determinants for CUT&RUN Success to Enable Predictive Protocol Design
ideas.description: This project moves beyond ad hoc validation to establish a predictive framework for CUT&RUN application. We will use a diverse panel of cells from key model organisms (including plants, insects, nematodes, and vertebrates) to systematically deconstruct the protocol in a high-throughput parameter space. The core output will be a quantitative model linking cellular features—such as cell wall structure, nuclear envelope composition, and endogenous nuclease activity—to optimal CUT&RUN conditions. This model will empower researchers to rationally adapt CUT&RUN for any new species or cell type by diagnosing potential failure points and prescribing tailored experimental parameters, transforming the technique from an empirical art into an engineered tool.

ideas.title: Mechanism-Informed Engineering of Modular Unidirectional Deletion Systems for Diverse Cloning Vectors
ideas.description: This project moves beyond a direct method transfer to first deconstruct the mechanistic basis of unidirectional deletion in the original M13 system. Using this understanding, we will design a suite of modular, orthogonal genetic tools (e.g., origin-specific nickases, targeted resolvases) that can be rationally matched to the replication and topological features of a target vector, such as a ColE1 plasmid or a Fosmid. The outcome will be a principled, adaptable platform for achieving precise, large-scale deletions across multiple cloning systems, with each vector class receiving a tailored implementation validated for efficiency and fidelity.

ideas.title: Inverse PCR-based amplification of unknown deletion breakpoints
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the need for prior knowledge of the deletion endpoint sequence. First, genomic DNA containing the deletion is digested with a restriction enzyme and self-ligated to form circular molecules. A primary inverse PCR is performed using outward-facing primers designed from known sequence internal to the deletion. A subsequent nested PCR with primers internal to the first amplicon increases specificity and yield. The final product can then be directly sequenced to characterize the precise, previously unknown deletion breakpoint.

ideas.title: Stochastic Truncation with Programmable Junction Synthesis (STPJS)
ideas.description: This system first uses a non-processive nuclease under controlled conditions to randomly truncate double-stranded DNA, creating a pool of molecules with heterogeneous ends. A synthetic oligonucleotide 'donor' is then introduced, containing a 3' region complementary to a desired internal target site (defining the deletion endpoint) and a 5' overhang complementary to the truncated DNA's remaining strand. A high-fidelity polymerase extends the truncated DNA using the donor as a template, synthesizing a precise new strand that bridges to the defined internal site, thereby creating a deletion with a controlled, user-defined junction. The final product is a defined deletion variant, transforming stochastic digestion into a precise, programmable editing outcome.

ideas.title: Sequence-aware, single-molecule mapping of ExoIII processivity for deterministic endpoint prediction
ideas.description: This project directly addresses the substrate-dependent variability in ExoIII digestion by combining two orthogonal approaches. First, single-molecule real-time observation of ExoIII processivity on diverse DNA templates identifies precise pausing and termination sites. Second, high-throughput sequencing of deletion endpoints from bulk reactions provides a statistical map of sequence-specific outcomes. By integrating these datasets, we build a predictive model that accounts for local DNA structure and sequence context, enabling the rational design of substrate sequences to achieve highly predictable deletion lengths for precision genome engineering applications.

ideas.title: Programmed Primer-Polymerase System for Direct, Fidelity-Checked Repair of Heterogeneous ExoIII Overhangs
ideas.description: This redesigned approach directly addresses the sequence heterogeneity of ExoIII-digested ends by employing a two-component system. First, a set of chimeric 'bridge primers' with a 3' universal sequence and a 5' random region anneals to the variable overhangs, creating a uniform template. Second, a high-fidelity polymerase with strong strand-displacement activity extends from this primer, displacing the original overhang and synthesizing across the gap. The system is validated using a next-generation sequencing-based assay to quantify repair efficiency, sequence bias, and mutation rates compared to the traditional S1 nuclease method.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Correlation for Direct Dejunction Amplification
ideas.description: This idea adapts a geophysical solution to a molecular biology problem. In seismology, the epicenter of an earthquake is pinpointed by correlating signal arrival times at multiple known stations (triangulation), bypassing the need to model the entire wave path. Adapted to genomics, the method involves using multiple PCR primer pairs designed to anneal to conserved sequences on either side of a suspected large deletion. By analyzing the pattern of which primer combinations yield products and the sizes of those products, one can directly 'triangulate' and amplify the precise deletion junction sequences. This approach eliminates the need for cloning digested genomic fragments, allowing for rapid, direct mapping of deletion endpoints analogous to how seismologists directly locate an earthquake's origin point.

ideas.title: Use Transposon-Mediated 'Molecular Sonar' for Genomic Structural Profiling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysical seismic imaging to transposon insertion sequencing. In seismic surveys, a controlled source generates waves whose reflections are captured by an array of receivers to map subsurface structures. Analogously, a custom transposon will act as the controlled genomic 'source'. It will be engineered to contain two fixed, outward-facing sequencing primer sites ('receivers') flanking its central element. Following random insertion into a target genome, the region will be amplified and subjected to paired-end sequencing from these fixed primer sites. Each sequencing read pair will capture the genomic sequences flanking both sides of the single insertion point, acting as a molecular echo. This adapted method generates paired structural data from each insertion event, enabling high-resolution profiling of local genomic architecture, such as detecting inversions, deletions, or tandem repeats, which is more informative than single-flank sequencing from traditional insertion sequencing approaches.

ideas.title: Engineered blunt-end ligation system with synthetic 3' terminus blockade
ideas.description: This project aims to replace the reliance on 3' overhangs by developing a two-component enzymatic system for vector protection. The first component is a high-fidelity DNA ligase engineered or selected for exclusive activity on perfectly blunt-ended DNA, eliminating the need for complementary overhangs. The second is a tailored terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT) variant that adds a single, synthetic nucleotide analog to the 3' end, creating a permanent chemical blockade against exonuclease digestion. The system will be validated by measuring cloning efficiency, vector recircularization rates, and resistance to purified exonucleases compared to traditional overhang-based methods.

ideas.title: A QC-Integrated, Self-Normalizing Automated CUT&RUN Platform for High-Confidence Genetic and Drug Screens
ideas.description: This system automates the CUT&RUN protocol in a 96- or 384-well format but crucially incorporates a mandatory, inline quality control checkpoint. After the pA-MNase cleavage and DNA release step, a small fraction of each well's supernatant is automatically transferred to a companion assay plate for immediate fluorometric DNA quantification. This data, collected before library preparation, is used to automatically flag wells with failed reactions and to calculate a normalization factor for downstream SPRI bead-based cleanup and library amplification. The result is a high-throughput screen where technical variability is actively measured and corrected, dramatically increasing the reliability of hit identification from genetic or compound perturbations.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Signal Fusion for Enhanced Sequence Reconstruction
ideas.description: This idea proposes to integrate the deletion method with emerging chemical or enzymatic sequencing techniques by drawing an analogy from geophysical imaging. In seismic tomography, the inverse problem of reconstructing subsurface images is solved by a joint inversion framework that fuses data from multiple geophysical methods (seismic, gravity, magnetic) to constrain and improve the final model. Adapted to genomics, this suggests developing a multi-modal sequencing framework. A computational joint analysis pipeline would be created to fuse the raw signal outputs from parallel applications of deletion sequencing, enzymatic sequencing (like nanopore), and emerging chemical methods (like fluorosequencing). By treating the DNA sequence as the target 'structure' and using the orthogonal error profiles and physical principles of each technique as complementary constraints, the pipeline would solve for the most probable consensus sequence. This approach mitigates the systematic biases and limitations inherent to any single sequencing chemistry, potentially yielding substantial gains in accuracy, especially for challenging regions like homopolymers or highly modified bases, analogous to how multi-geophysical surveys resolve complex subsurface features.

ideas.title: In Situ Enzymatic Tagging System for Universal Protein Targeting
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fundamental limitation of requiring pre-engineered tags by developing a two-component platform. First, a programmable enzyme (e.g., an engineered ligase or transpeptidase) is designed to recognize a short, ubiquitous peptide sequence or post-translational modification found on many native proteins, delivering a universal synthetic affinity tag specifically to that site. Second, this covalently attached tag is then bound by a highly consistent, off-the-shelf detection or therapeutic effector molecule. This enables the targeting of wild-type proteins in their native cellular environment, dramatically expanding applicability to diagnostics, research, and therapeutics without genetic pre-modification of the target.

ideas.title: Microfluidic CUT&RUN for clinical biopsies using forensic DNA extraction principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from forensic science, where specialized microfluidic devices and silica-based magnetic bead systems were developed to extract trace DNA from minute, degraded, or contaminated samples (e.g., touch DNA, ancient remains). By applying this analogy, we propose to design a closed, miniaturized CUT&RUN workflow using a microfluidic chip that integrates tissue lysis, chromatin extraction, antibody binding, and enzymatic cleavage in nanoliter volumes. The chip would employ forensic-optimized silica-coated magnetic beads for efficient chromatin capture from fixed or frozen clinical biopsy fragments, dramatically reducing sample and reagent requirements while minimizing handling losses, thereby enabling robust epigenomic profiling from extremely limited clinical tissue samples.

ideas.title: Modeling In Situ Cleavage Background as Astronomical Sky Subtraction
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that non-specific cleavage noise must be minimized at the experimental level, instead accepting it as an unavoidable background akin to the sky glow in astronomy. Drawing an analogy from astronomical sky subtraction techniques, we propose a computational method where the experimental data is first analyzed to define regions analogous to 'blank sky'—areas where only non-specific cleavage occurs. A spatially and/or sequence-context-aware model is then fitted to this background signal, capturing its statistical properties and variation. This model is subsequently subtracted from the total observed cleavage signal across the entire dataset (e.g., genome or tissue sample), isolating the residual signal attributable to specific, targeted cleavage events. This approach transforms the problem from one of experimental elimination to one of computational separation, potentially increasing sensitivity and quantification accuracy for the specific signal.

ideas.title: Adapting Error-Correcting Code Pruning for Vector System Optimization
ideas.description: This idea extends the unidirectional deletion method by drawing an analogy from digital communications. The analogous problem is managing complexity and correcting errors in signal transmission, solved by the Viterbi algorithm's path pruning. The adapted research idea applies this pruning logic to plasmid and other cloning systems. By initiating parallel recombination events and then applying a sequence-specific negative selection to delete all incorrect or incomplete vector assemblies, the method would systematically 'prune' unwanted molecular pathways. This creates an error-correcting, high-fidelity cloning workflow that guarantees output of only the intended construct, significantly improving efficiency for complex assemblies beyond M13 systems.

ideas.title: Modeling ExoIII Digestion as a Catalytic Reaction Network for Endpoint Prediction
ideas.description: This idea applies the problem-solving framework from chemical reaction engineering and enzyme kinetics to the challenge of unpredictable ExoIII deletion endpoints. The analogous field uses formal kinetic models (e.g., Michaelis-Menten, models for processive enzymes) to predict reaction progress by quantifying how factors like substrate concentration and environment affect catalytic constants. The adapted research idea is to model ExoIII digestion as a processive enzymatic reaction network. By systematically studying how parameters such as enzyme-to-DNA ratio, ionic strength, temperature, and DNA structure influence the digestion rate constant, we can collect empirical data to build a predictive kinetic model. This model would then allow for the calculation of expected deletion endpoints based on specific input reaction conditions, transforming a semi-stochastic process into a precision tool.

ideas.title: A Programmable, Microfluidic 'Synthesis Robot' for Automated, Timed Multi-Step Sample Processing
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from automated synthesis platforms in organic chemistry and pharmaceutical development, this idea proposes adapting the principles of programmable flow chemistry reactors to automate timed digestions and sample processing. In the analogous field, systems use precisely controlled fluidic networks to sequentially deliver reagents, manage reaction times, and perform purification steps autonomously for multi-step chemical syntheses. The adapted research idea is to develop a closed, programmable microfluidic 'synthesis robot' for biological samples. This platform would utilize integrated valves, pumps, and sensors within a disposable cartridge to autonomously execute complete, timed processing protocols—such as adding enzymes, incubating for precise durations, quenching reactions, and performing clean-up steps—minimizing manual intervention, improving reproducibility, and enabling complex, hands-off sample preparation workflows.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Automated Sample Processing with Integrated Pre-Analytical Quality Control
ideas.description: This system combines robotic fluidic handling for timed digestions with an intelligent, sensor-equipped front-end module that manages sample intake. Every sample tube is automatically scanned and verified against an experiment manifest upon loading, while in-line sensors check for correct volume and the absence of gross particulates or bubbles. Only validated samples proceed into the automated processing workflow, where every aliquot, reagent addition, and incubation step is tracked and recorded. This design ensures that automation enhances not just throughput and timing precision, but also the fundamental reliability and reproducibility of the entire pre-analytical phase.
